text: AI, like other empirical disciplines, can be studied using axiomatic principles. This work lays out postulates defining what a versatile, human-equivalent AI system must accomplish. We investigate the foundational approach of deep learning, scrutinizing both overt and implicit presumptions underlying current deep learning scholarship. By integrating model sophistication, broad applicability, and real-world utility, deep learning methodology addresses shortcomings inherent in classical machine learning approaches. The method has achieved remarkable success through the combination of function fitting techniques, reasonable assumptions about irreducibility, and the computational efficiency of backpropagation-based methods. We analyze these distinguishing strengths of deep learning while also documenting its documented shortcomings and breakdown scenarios. Finally, we propose strategies for adapting deep learning methodology to satisfy the requirements of universal AI systems, particularly regarding structured design and brain-inspired processing frameworks. Additionally, we connect deep learning advances to contemporary developments in computational neuroscience.
label: ai

text: Deep representation learning through unsupervised methods represents one of the field's most effective approaches. Foundational architectures such as Restricted Boltzman machines, sparse coding, regularized auto-encoders, and convolutional neural networks have shaped the development of deep learning. This work introduces a novel architectural component termed distributed random models. The technique implements a variant of the product of experts framework with two distinguishing characteristics: (i) each expert component manages its own collection of hidden units, with varying cardinalities across different experts; (ii) every expert applies k-center clustering where cluster centers derive from uniformly sampled data points, and generates outputs (hidden units) represented as sparse encodings containing only similarity measurements from the closest neighboring points. We examine how this contribution relates to established deep learning architectures, related research directions, and existing methodological approaches. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that the resulting deep architecture produces superior representations compared to deep belief networks while requiring substantially reduced training time to scale to significantly larger models than deep belief networks allow.

text: We introduce a remarkably straightforward neural architecture designed for unsupervised nonlinear dimensionality reduction called deep distributed random samplings, functioning as an ensemble of independent unsupervised bootstrap techniques. text: While deep learning approaches to speech enhancement have shown promising results in challenging acoustic settings, their effectiveness diminishes significantly as the distance between speakers and microphones increases, since acoustic signals attenuate rapidly as they travel through air. This work introduces \textit{deep ad-hoc beamforming} to tackle the challenge of processing speech from distant sources. The approach incorporates two innovative elements. First, it merges \textit{ad-hoc microphone arrays}—composed of arbitrarily positioned microphones that work cooperatively—with deep-learning-powered multichannel speech enhancement, substantially reducing the likelihood of encountering far-field acoustic scenarios. Second, it introduces a novel challenge to deep-learning-based multichannel enhancement: \textit{channel selection}—a technique that identifies and groups microphones near the speech source into a focused array using a selection mechanism. This mechanism operates in two steps: a deep neural network initially assesses the quality of the speech signal captured by each microphone, followed by aggregation of microphones exhibiting both high speech quality and substantial inter-channel signal coherence into a localized array. We developed multiple variants of the selection mechanism, ranging from straightforward single-channel selection to sophisticated machine-learning approaches. Extensive evaluations were performed across configurations with unknown, unpredictable, and distant speaker positions. Experimental findings demonstrate substantial improvements over existing deep-learning-based speech enhancement approaches across both distributed noise reverberant conditions and localized noise reverberant conditions.

text:
Deep learning currently occupies a central place in research efforts. This paper introduces a conceptual model highlighting what makes current methods work and potentially enabling future innovation.

Backpropagation serves as the foundation for virtually all deep learning techniques, functioning as nothing more than distributed gradient calculation across a neural network. The proposed framework therefore naturally incorporates two primary elements: (i) tools from game theory, enabling formalization of distributed problem-solving; and (ii) systems for exchanging information, allowing us to monitor how zeroth and first-order data moves through networks. text: In typical deep neural network training, a common presumption holds that all training instances present equivalent learning challenges. text: Among the various machine learning approaches used to train deep neural networks today, Stochastic Gradient Descent stands out as one of the most widely adopted techniques. text: The multilayer bootstrap network (MBN) has recently shown strong results in unsupervised dimensionality reduction, successfully generating efficient representations on popular benchmarks such as MNIST and RCV1. Yet like other bootstrap approaches, MBN suffers from substantial computational overhead during inference. This work introduces a general unsupervised model compression strategy designed to address the efficiency limitations inherent in unsupervised bootstrap methods. The strategy operates by treating the bootstrap model and its downstream task as a unified system, then training a supervised model to directly map inputs from the bootstrap component to the final application outputs, thereby bypassing the original pipeline. We instantiate this approach with compressive MBN, which pairs MBN as the unsupervised bootstrap component with a deep neural network (DNN) serving as the supervised mapping learner. Experiments on MNIST demonstrate that compressive MBN preserves MBN's high accuracy while achieving speedups exceeding several thousand times during inference. This finding indicates that the proposed method successfully combines the representational strengths of MBN in unsupervised contexts with the computational advantages of DNNs in supervised settings, resulting in a technique that is both accurate and efficient for unsupervised learning tasks.

text: Accelerated gradient descent and related techniques from convex optimization serve as fundamental components in modern deep learning systems. Yet their remarkable practical performance remains poorly understood, given that neural networks lack convexity and theoretical guarantees from convex settings do not extend to them. This work establishes the first formal connection between online convex optimization and the backpropagation algorithm used in convolutional architectures. The approach begins by introducing circadian games—a generalization of standard convex games that preserves their desirable convergence characteristics. The central contribution demonstrates that backpropagation in convolutional networks corresponds precisely to executing a circadian game. From this equivalence, it becomes clear that the waking-regret accumulated by individual game players (which map to network units) directly determines how quickly the entire network converges. We then discuss several consequences of this framework: (i) we provide a game-theoretic perspective on the feature representations that emerge during neural network training, (ii) we introduce a unit-level learning mechanism suitable for integration into modern deep models, and (iii) we develop an adaptive strategy for choosing network components by leveraging bandit-based selection algorithms to determine which players become active at each iteration.
label: ai

text:
Decision tree approaches have long been favored by practitioners, yet many researchers seek straightforward algorithms that can match tree performance. In 2010, Ping Li (UAI'10) introduced "abc-robust-logitboost," which he tested against competing supervised learning approaches using benchmark datasets commonly employed in deep learning research. We present here a collection of "tunable GMM kernels"—straightforward techniques that achieve competitive results comparable to tree-based approaches on identical datasets. The "abc-robust-logitboost" method represented a substantial advance over the original "GDBT" by: (a) introducing a tree-splitting formula grounded in second-order information derived from loss function gradients; and (b) formulating new derivative specifications tailored to multi-class classification problems.

A 2017 investigation had demonstrated that the "generalized min-max" (GMM) kernel performed favorably relative to the "radial-basis function" (RBF) kernel. Nevertheless, our experiments reveal that the standard GMM kernel fails to rival tree-based methods when tested against deep learning benchmark datasets. Because the original GMM kernel lacks tunable parameters, we propose enhanced variants by introducing adjustable parameters through multiple mechanisms. The three fundamental single-parameter variations—namely the "$e$GMM kernel," "$p$GMM kernel," and "$\gamma$GMM kernel"—represent our core innovations. Our experimental results across numerous classification problems demonstrate their effectiveness. Additionally, these basic kernel variants can be merged together to further enhance predictive performance.

text: Networks built on recurrent principles possess the capacity to learn temporal patterns and leverage historical activation data across virtually any time interval. Yet standard gradient-based optimization approaches frequently encounter practical obstacles when attempting to capture dependencies that span extended periods. We present an innovative training strategy employing multiple synchronized cloned networks that operate in parallel, all maintaining identical parameters while processing inputs at distinct temporal offsets and preserving separate activation trajectories. The training procedure works by iteratively applying weight modifications to the ensemble of parallel clones while progressively extending the activation record. This mechanism enables incompatibilities to move upward through organizational levels, flowing from immediate-term patterns toward extended-term patterns until resolution occurs. We demonstrate this parallel clones technique and the resulting hierarchical incompatibility resolution using a character-focused deep recurrent network that learns to reproduce a passage from Herman Melville's Moby Dick.

text: Previous work (citations: zhang2014nonlinear, zhang2014nonlinear2) conceptualized machine learning through the lens of coding and dimensionality reduction, introducing an unsupervised approach called deep distributed random samplings (DDRS). This work builds upon that foundation by extending DDRS into the supervised learning domain. The central contribution involves incorporating class labels directly into the coding mechanism, achieved by assigning multiple output nodes to each center that specify its corresponding class. While the resulting supervised technique shares certain characteristics with random forests (citation: breiman2001random), several important distinctions merit attention: (i) Our approach evaluates connections between subsets and the complete training set at each level, whereas random forests independently construct individual trees from isolated data subsets. (ii) We progressively reduce network width through decreased sampling, contrasting with random forests' approach of gradually narrowing through class merging. (iii) Training proceeds sequentially from lower to upper layers, differing from random forests' top-down tree construction via recursive splitting. (iv) Target outputs are encoded implicitly within sparse representations in our method, while random forests encode them by preserving class information at activated nodes. Consequently, our approach offers a cleaner, more direct, and potentially superior option, despite both techniques relying fundamentally on identical building blocks: randomization and nearest neighbor optimization. This document serves as a preliminary record to establish priority for the supervised extension concept referenced in earlier works, with comprehensive experimental validation to follow in due course.

text: We introduce a speaker verification back-end grounded in linear regression methodology. Linear regression operates as a straightforward linear model designed to reduce the gap between predicted values and actual targets through mean squared error minimization, employing an analytical solution where targets represent the true class indicator sequences of speech samples. We employ this regression-based approach to construct speaker representations derived from various front-end systems, then assess speaker model resemblance using cosine similarity as our scoring mechanism. To examine how well this approach performs in practice, we develop three distinct speaker verification frameworks incorporating different front-end architectures: one pairing Gaussian mixture models with i-vectors (GMM/i-vector), another combining deep neural networks with i-vectors (DNN/i-vector), and a third using d-vectors. Experimental validation conducted on NIST speaker recognition benchmarks demonstrates that our proposed technique consistently surpasses established baseline methods—specifically within-class covariance normalization, linear discriminant analysis, and probabilistic linear discriminant analysis—across all three front-end configurations.

text: The past ten years have witnessed extraordinary developments in artificial intelligence, primarily driven by the resurgence of Deep Learning as a foundational approach. Deep Learning facilitates the handling of vast datasets through simplified artificial neural structures modeled after brain function. Yet a competing perspective argues that the brain manipulates information rather than mere data, a conceptual divide that has obstructed artificial intelligence advancement for considerable time. This work introduces Integrated Information as a framework intended to bridge this gap. I conceptualize integrated information as a fusion of two distinct elements: physical information, which encompasses data manipulation, and semantic information, which furnishes meaning to physical information. From this perspective, intelligence emerges as the outcome of information manipulation. Following this reasoning further, information manipulation need not rely on human neurological architecture. Indeed, microorganisms such as bacteria and amoebas demonstrate intelligent capabilities absent any brain structure whatsoever. This observation fundamentally undermines the necessity for artificial intelligence systems to replicate human neural complexity! The work examines this transformation in how artificial intelligence systems might be philosophically conceived and engineered.

text: The fundamental mechanism through which deep neural networks (DNN) achieve abstraction involves the demodulation of linear filter outputs. text: Techniques originating in convex optimization play a fundamental role as components within algorithms for deep learning. Yet the underlying reasons for their observed empirical performance remain poorly understood, particularly because contemporary convolutional architectures—which incorporate rectified linear units alongside max-pooling operations—lack both smoothness and convexity properties. Consequently, conventional theoretical guarantees fail to hold. This work delivers the initial convergence rate analysis for gradient-based optimization applied to rectifier convolutional networks. The analytical strategy leverages the distinctive design of such networks, which involves binary switching mechanisms (active or inactive states) layered atop a foundational linear structure. This framework extends naturally to encompassing max-pooling, dropout, and maxout architectures—that is, to the specific network variants demonstrating superior practical performance. The crucial innovation involves introducing gated games, a generalization of conventional convex games maintaining analogous convergence characteristics while modeling the gating mechanism inherent in rectifier units. The primary contribution establishes that rectifier convolutional networks approach critical points with convergence rates determined by the gated-regret metric across the network's component units. Several important implications follow from this central finding: (i) a game-theoretic interpretation of feature representations constructed by neural networks; (ii) a sublinear-regret training procedure for neural network optimization; and (iii) a rigorous framework for studying conditional computation phenomena in neural networks applicable to contemporary attention-based architectures.

text:
Training deep neural networks typically relies on stochastic gradient descent (SGD), an approach that reduces the objective function through coarse gradient approximations that only converge toward true gradient values on average. Conventional techniques such as momentum and ADAM focus exclusively on directional movement and ignore how far the optimization stands from critical points, thereby discarding potentially useful information embedded in the accumulated gradient history and frequently getting stuck on suboptimal plateaus. Second-order methods could leverage these overlooked opportunities, yet they face prohibitive computational demands, numerical instability issues, and a tendency to converge toward undesirable locations like saddle points due to failing to account for the signs of curvature measurements (represented by Hessian eigenvalues).

The Saddle-free Newton method (SFN) stands out as an uncommon approach tackling this problem—it reverses saddle attraction into repulsion and has demonstrated significant gains in final optimization results. Nevertheless, SFN disregards stochasticity when handling second-order information, concentrates on Krylov subspaces for computational stability, and demands expensive eigendecomposition operations.

Building on SFN's strengths while avoiding its limitations, economical approaches are introduced for capturing these underutilized advantages. Since second-order behavior amounts to linear relationships in first derivatives, one can effectively infer it from noisy gradient sequences via least squares regression adapted for online use: progressively downweighting earlier gradients. Principal component analysis of recent noisy gradients reveals the statistically meaningful subspace—implementable online by incrementally rotating the basis toward emerging gradients and systematically phasing out outdated directions in favor of current ones. Eigendecomposition itself becomes an online procedure: periodic QR decomposition steps maintain a diagonal Hessian representation. Concurrently, standard gradient descent operates within the space orthogonal to the modeled second-order region.
label: ai

text: The influential work by Maynard Smith and Szathm\'ary, titled The Major Transitions in Evolution, examines eight pivotal moments in life's development and reveals an underlying principle connecting them. Across these moments, smaller units merged to create larger wholes through mechanisms of symbiosis and mutual aid. We offer here a computational model simulating the evolution of entities that incorporates symbiosis across multiple selection tiers. Within our model, entities gain fitness through successive pairwise matchups using the Immigration Game, a variant of Conway's Game of Life designed for two competitors. The processes of mutation, replication, and symbiosis function as external operations separate from the Immigration Game itself. By keeping these processes independent of the game mechanics, we gain the ability to adjust them deliberately and assess resulting changes. The simulation operates through four sequential levels, with each tier expanding upon the one before it. The initial tier creates basic asexual replication, the subsequent tier refines asexual replication further, the third implements sexual replication, and the final introduces symbiosis. Our findings demonstrate that introducing modest levels of symbiosis alongside the other tiers produces substantial gains in population fitness. We propose that this symbiotic framework offers valuable understanding for both biological and cultural evolution, while also holding promise for practical uses in computational evolution, particularly for developing neural network training methods.

text:
Vitamin B12, siderophores, and glycans exist in multiple molecular forms. Bacteria require diverse receptors to capture these different variants. The intestinal bacterium Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron illustrates this diversity, possessing three distinct receptors for vitamin B12 variants and 88 receptors capable of binding various glycan types. The composition of these receptor suites reveals fundamental mechanisms driving cellular evolution.

Nutrient competition tends to specialize each organism toward particular nutrient categories. While certain gut bacteria access only limited carbohydrate types, others like B. thetaiotaomicron possess the metabolic capacity to break down numerous distinct complex glycans. By examining diverse nutrients across different environments and analyzing genome sequences, researchers can test theories regarding receptor diversity. A second critical evolutionary driver involves nutrient scarcity and abundance cycles. These cyclical changes amplify the significance of biological sensors that monitor external conditions and modulate receptor availability accordingly. Many bacterial species fine-tune receptor production based on fluctuating carbohydrate abundance. Similarly, some bacteria alter their uptake receptor levels when siderophore availability changes. The mechanism by which cells leverage sensory data to orchestrate responses to environmental shifts represents a key question in regulatory architecture—one that intersects with concerns central to control systems theory and machine intelligence.

Control systems theory offers analytical frameworks for understanding environmental volatility alongside sensor and response apparatus design. Contemporary machine learning research, particularly deep learning methodologies, investigates regulatory network structure and how intricate control systems encode and categorize environmental conditions. This discussion highlights the parallel design challenges present across cellular evolution, control systems theory, and machine intelligence, connecting these frameworks to concrete, verifiable predictions concerning bacterial assimilation of B12, siderophores, and glycans.

text: The process of inductive concept learning involves categorizing examples into a finite collection of categories. Some researchers have focused specifically on the expenses associated with classification mistakes. However, relatively few have explored the broader range of cost considerations. This work seeks to develop a structured framework organizing the multiple cost dimensions inherent in inductive concept learning. Such a framework could serve as a useful tool for categorizing existing research on cost-aware learning approaches. We anticipate this effort will motivate the research community to more thoroughly investigate the full spectrum of costs relevant to inductive concept learning.

text:
We investigate the application of "sign α-stable random projections" (with 0<α≤2) for developing fundamental data analysis techniques suited to large-scale machine learning contexts, including classification, regression, clustering, and nearest-neighbor retrieval. Once data undergoes transformation via sign stable random projections, the resulting inner products of the transformed data can approximate different classes of nonlinear kernels based on the particular value of α. Consequently, this strategy offers an efficient means to implement nonlinear algorithms with computational requirements comparable to linear methods. For α=2, the associated nonlinear kernel is recognized as the arc-cosine kernel. When α=1, the method approximates the arc-cos-χ² kernel (subject to specific conditions). As α approaches 0 from above, it relates to the resemblance kernel.

From a practical standpoint, sign α-stable random projections represent a technique ready for deployment in large-scale learning scenarios, where α functions as a straightforward hyperparameter to adjust. A notable gap exists in prior research: comprehensive empirical validation demonstrating the utility of sign stable random projections, particularly when α differs from 2 or 1. This work addresses that gap through systematic evaluation across numerous classification benchmarks. We specifically conduct direct comparisons between sign stable random projections and the recently introduced "0-bit consistent weighted sampling (CWS)" methodology (Li 2015).

text: Scholarly publications typically require authors to supply between five and fifteen important terms, which are featured on the opening page of their submissions. Because these terms frequently consist of multiple words grouped together, we refer to them as keyphrases rather than simple keywords. This paper explores the surprisingly diverse range of applications where keyphrases prove valuable. Contemporary commercial tools—including Microsoft Word 97 and Verity Search 97—now incorporate built-in mechanisms for automatically identifying keyphrases within documents. We address the challenge of automatically identifying keyphrases from documents by formulating it as a supervised machine learning problem. We conceptualize each document as a collection of phrases that an algorithm must learn to distinguish between those that are and are not legitimate keyphrases. Our initial experiments employ the C4.5 decision tree learning method for this classification task. A second series of experiments utilizes the GenEx algorithm, which we specifically created for this purpose. The third experimental phase compares GenEx's effectiveness in generating metadata against the capabilities of Microsoft Word 97. The fourth and concluding set of experiments assesses GenEx's performance on highlighting tasks, benchmarked against Verity Search 97. Findings from our experiments demonstrate that purpose-built learning algorithms like GenEx outperform both general-purpose machine learning approaches such as C4.5 and the algorithm-based techniques employed in existing commercial applications like Word 97 and Search 97 at producing quality keyphrases.

text: We recast two pivotal capacity metrics from statistical learning theory—specifically empirical VC-entropy and empirical Rademacher complexity—using information-theoretic foundations. Our analysis reveals that these capacity metrics effectively enumerate the quantity of hypotheses regarding a given dataset that a learning algorithm rejects upon selecting the lowest empirical risk classifier from its available set. text:
A minimum of three distinct frameworks exist for measuring information. The first framework, known as algorithmic information or Kolmogorov complexity, represents events as character sequences and employs a universal Turing machine to assess how much information a string contains by determining the minimal length of code needed to generate it. The second framework, Shannon information, conceptualizes events as part of larger collections and evaluates the informational value obtained from observing a particular event by counting how many other possible events have been eliminated. The third framework, statistical learning theory, has developed capacity measures that partially govern the anticipated error rate of prediction models. These capacity measures reflect the assumptions regarding upcoming observations that learning systems encode into their prediction models.

This paper introduces a novel approach to measuring information called effective information, which creates connections between algorithmic information and Shannon information, while also bridging both of these to capacity measures found in statistical learning theory. The paper first presents this measure and demonstrates that it functions as a non-universal version of Kolmogorov complexity. Subsequently, the authors employ it to establish fundamental capacity measures within statistical learning theory: empirical VC-entropy and empirical Rademacher complexity. A useful consequence of this approach involves characterizing the predictive utility of a learning algorithm according to the quantity of possible explanations it rules out, assessed through two distinct counting methods corresponding to the two capacity measures. The paper additionally examines connections between effective information and related concepts such as information gain, Shannon information, and mutual information.
label: ai

text: This position paper contends that the evolutionary computation community harbors considerable confusion regarding the Baldwin effect. These conceptual errors generally fall into two broad categories. First, many researchers mistakenly assume that the Baldwin effect describes the advantageous interaction arising when a population undergoes evolution while individual members possess learning capabilities. However, this perspective captures only partial truth. The complete picture is considerably richer and more nuanced. In reality, the Baldwin effect examines both the advantages and disadvantages associated with individual learning across the lifespan within populations subject to evolutionary change. While numerous scholars have concentrated on positive outcomes, considerable insights emerge from examining negative outcomes as well. This paper delineates both dimensions of this phenomenon and identifies ten distinct advantages and disadvantages connected to learning during an organism's lifetime within an evolving population. Second, a series of misapprehensions surround how the Baldwin effect relates to the Lamarckian concept of heritable acquired traits. The Baldwin effect operates through fundamentally different mechanisms than Lamarckism. For most evolutionary computing applications, employing Lamarckian procedures does not yield superior results compared to Baldwinian procedures. Additionally, Lamarckian inheritance provides a less accurate framework for understanding memetic or cultural transmission than the Baldwin effect does.

text:
A fundamental challenge in machine learning involves dealing with unknown object rotations, particularly relevant to tasks like image classification. Similarly, when comparing the shapes of objects such as chemical compounds, a straightforward technique becomes impractical because it demands expensive computations involving sequential rotation adjustments and assessments.

Rotation invariants serve as valuable instruments in these contexts, enabling practitioners to extract characteristics that describe shape independently of rotational orientation. These characteristics prove beneficial for applications ranging from locating similar patterns despite rotation, to efficiently determining shape resemblance—useful in applications like drug discovery—or incorporating shape-based descriptors into machine learning systems. The conventional solution employs rotationally invariant spherical or cylindrical harmonics, which derive from polynomial formulations on a sphere. However, this method yields relatively few invariants; specifically, it generates only a single invariant per polynomial degree.

This work examines a universal methodology for generating arbitrarily extensive collections of rotation invariants applicable to polynomials of degree $D$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. This approach offers up to $O(n^D)$ linearly independent invariants in contrast to merely $O(D)$ invariants that conventional techniques provide. Furthermore, such a framework could potentially constitute a complete collection: delivering not merely necessary conditions, but also sufficient criteria for determining whether two forms differ solely through rotation (and reflective transformation).

text: The foundation of statistical theory rests on parametric density estimation using models such as Gaussian distributions. However, contemporary machine learning demands efficient methods for approximating substantially more intricate densities, yet conventional approaches like maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) prove computationally prohibitive. This discussion explores cost-effective alternatives to density estimation, including straightforward techniques such as polynomial fitting (or expanding via Fourier series) applied to observed data, wherein the necessary coefficients are derived through simple averaging of polynomial terms (or trigonometric functions) across the sample. Random projections using 1-bit quantization have become widely adopted for scalable similarity search and statistical learning applications on large-scale data. The probability that the signs of $x$ and $y$ match is given by ${Pr}\left(sgn(x)=sgn(y)\right) = 1-\frac{\cos^{-1}\rho}{\pi}$, where $\rho = \rho(u,v)$ denotes the cosine similarity between the vectors.

When working with nonnegative data, we propose a particularly promising estimator constructed from $E\left(y_- 1_{x\geq 0} + y_+ 1_{x<0}\right)$ together with its normalized variant. In the high-similarity regime ($\rho\rightarrow1$), the asymptotic variance of our recommended method is merely $\frac{4}{3\pi} \approx 0.4$ relative to the variance associated with 1-bit methods. text: This paper presents Latent Relational Analysis (LRA), a technique designed to assess the similarity between relations. The methodology has broad relevance across numerous domains, such as data extraction, resolving word ambiguity, cross-language translation, and document retrieval. Relational similarity refers to the degree of correspondence shared by relations themselves, whereas attributional similarity concerns correspondence between properties or features. Two words exhibiting substantial attributional similarity are typically termed synonyms. Conversely, when two word pairs demonstrate high relational similarity, we describe their relations as analogous—for instance, mason relates to stone in much the same way carpenter relates to wood. Previous research into semantic similarity has predominantly focused on attributional similarity. The Vector Space Model (VSM), a framework from information retrieval, has recently been adapted to evaluate relational similarity, yielding a 47% accuracy rate when tested against 374 undergraduate-level analogy questions. Within this VSM framework, a word pair's relation is represented through a vector capturing the frequency of predetermined patterns drawn from extensive text corpora. LRA enhances the VSM methodology through three key modifications: (1) patterns are extracted directly from the corpus rather than being fixed in advance, (2) Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is employed to refine frequency distributions (a technique similarly utilized in Latent Semantic Analysis), and (3) machine-generated antonyms help examine alternative representations of word pairs. LRA attains 56% accuracy on the same 374 analogy questions, a result statistically indistinguishable from the typical human performance of 57%. For the parallel task of categorizing noun-modifier connections, LRA demonstrates comparable improvements relative to VSM while requiring less textual data.

text: Similarity between entities can manifest in at least two distinct ways. One form involves matching between structural connections—what we term relational similarity—while the other concerns matching between inherent qualities, known as attributional similarity. Two words displaying strong attributional similarity are typically referred to as synonyms. Conversely, when two word pairs demonstrate strong relational similarity, we describe their connections as analogous; for instance, mason:stone and carpenter:wood share an analogous relationship. This work presents Latent Relational Analysis (LRA), a computational approach designed to quantify relational similarity. The method holds considerable promise for practical uses spanning information extraction, disambiguation of word meanings, and document retrieval systems. The Vector Space Model (VSM), traditionally employed in retrieval tasks, was recently modified to assess relational similarity, yielding performance of 47% when evaluated against 374 university-level word analogy multiple-choice items. Under the VSM framework, the relationship connecting two words gets represented as a frequency vector derived from predetermined linguistic patterns found in extensive text collections. LRA builds upon this foundation through three key enhancements: (1) patterns are extracted algorithmically rather than predefined, (2) Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) applies noise reduction to frequency information, and (3) machine-generated synonyms enable exploration of alternative word pair formulations. Testing on the same 374 analogy problems, LRA reaches 56% accuracy, a result statistically comparable to human performance at 57%. When applied to the parallel task of organizing semantic relations into categories, LRA demonstrates analogous improvements relative to the baseline VSM approach.

text:
Extending the concept of Singular Value Decomposition beyond matrices to handle multidimensional data structures yields higher-order tensor decompositions. The classical SVD technique has proven remarkably effective across numerous domains including information retrieval, recommendation systems, natural language processing, and computer vision. Nevertheless, SVD operates exclusively on two-dimensional data matrices (two modes), making it unsuitable for applications involving data with three or more dimensions, which demand more advanced tensor decomposition methods instead.

This work compares four distinct approaches to decomposing higher-order tensors: Higher-Order Singular Value Decomposition (HO-SVD), Higher-Order Orthogonal Iteration (HOOI), Slice Projection (SP), and Multislice Projection (MP). The investigation examines three critical performance aspects—execution duration, computational resources (including memory and storage capacity), and accuracy of tensor reconstruction—across diverse experimental scenarios. Our findings demonstrate that conventional versions of HO-SVD and HOOI struggle with scalability when applied to large-scale tensors, primarily because memory demands escalate rapidly. Based on these results, we suggest employing HOOI when tensor dimensions fit within available memory constraints, while MP proves more suitable for processing substantially larger tensors.

text:
John Pierce introduced model-based coding in 1961 as a powerful technique for minimizing the amount of data requiring transmission across distances or through online channels while maintaining complete fidelity. This approach offers distinct advantages over conventional compression techniques because both the sending party ("Alice") and receiving party ("Bob") possess identical formal descriptions of the data structure being communicated. Since both parties share this structural knowledge, substantially fewer bits must be exchanged to accomplish lossless transfer of information between them. Early experiments suggest that model-based coding may require transmitting merely 6% or less of the data volume necessary when traditional compression approaches are employed.

Converting Pierce's concept into practical application remained unfeasible for decades. However, recent advances in the "SP theory of intelligence" and its implementation through the "SP computer model" now suggest genuine possibilities for executing the three essential operations required: teaching a computer to autonomously acquire a grammar describing the transmissible data using sufficiently advanced hardware independent of Alice and Bob; allowing Alice to express any particular data instance according to this learned grammar; and enabling Bob to reverse Alice's encoding using the grammar to recover the original information. The feasibility of developing these functionalities to practical levels suitable for managing realistically sized datasets appears achievable within acceptable timeframes.

When a registered user seeks access and declares their identity, they must submit a new biometric sample. The system then retrieves the auxiliary message corresponding to the claimed identity and reconstructs an approximation of the secret key, which is subsequently matched against the authentic key of that individual. Should the reconstructed and original keys align, the authentication is granted; if they diverge, access is denied.

Naturally, the design of the auxiliary message encoder faces a fundamental dilemma between two competing objectives. On one side, the encoding mechanism must capture sufficient information about the legitimate user's identity to successfully validate them during authentication. Conversely, it must not reveal too much, since excessive information would enable malicious actors to circumvent the system and gain unauthorized entry. Therefore, an effective encoder must balance two distinct failure modes: the false rejection (FR) rate, where legitimate users are wrongly denied, and the false acceptance (FA) rate, where imposters are incorrectly granted access.

This study examines the relationship between the random coding FR error exponent and the maximum achievable FA error exponent. We evaluate and compare two code ensemble categories: those operating at constant rates and those adjusting rates dynamically. Our findings indicate that variable-rate codes substantially outperform constant-rate alternatives. Additionally, we establish the ideal rate functions applicable to each code category. Finally, we assess privacy concerns and information disclosure risks associated with both constant-rate and variable-rate implementations.

text: Selecting an appropriate similarity metric for normalizing Author Co-citation Analysis (ACA) becomes even more intricate when one takes into account the distinction between symmetrical co-citation matrices (or more broadly, co-occurrence matrices) and their corresponding asymmetrical citation matrices (or occurrence matrices). Should one seek to reverse the system's trajectory and retrieve it for examination at infinity, the acceleration needed to accomplish this generates Unruh radiation. Such radiation can perturb the information-carrying components within the system, thereby degrading the quality and reliability of that information. We identify the particular distance from the horizon at which information becomes practically irretrievable through this mechanism, finding that this critical distance varies with the system's energy gap and interaction strength. To discern underlying patterns, we employ the richly structured ground states of BPS configurations within quantum gravity frameworks (specifically string theory) as our information repository. Analysis reveals that for these systems, the threshold distance below which Unruh radiation dominates is precisely the geometric mean between the black hole's characteristic radius and the radius of an extremal black hole matching the BPS state's quantum attributes. These findings propose that within gravitational theories, information should not be treated as a fixed property embedded within any given system, but rather characterized by the degree to which different observers can gain access to it.

text: For decades, the black-hole information conundrum has captivated the interest of both mathematicians and theoretical physicists alike. text:

text: Across many scientific fields, the core mathematical equations appear to display a striking uniformity in their underlying structure. Evolution through natural selection augments information in a manner parallel to how Bayesian inference expands information content. Both thermodynamic principles and standard probability distribution families demonstrate maximum entropy growth, which manifests mathematically as information reduction. Mechanics in physics adheres to trajectories that optimize Fisher information gain. These information-based formulations typically admit interpretations resembling Newton's equilibrium of force and acceleration—distinguishing between what directly drives transformation and what opposes change relative to the reference system. This constellation of loose correspondences suggests an overarching mathematical framework. I propose that the Price equation captures this fundamental universal architecture. At its core, the Price equation characterizes transformation as variation occurring between two assemblies. One aspect of this transformation captures how the distribution of elements shifts while their associated attributes remain unchanged. The complementary aspect captures how attributes themselves evolve while element distribution stays fixed. This decoupling of distribution from attribute mirrors Shannon's distinction between symbol frequency and symbol interpretation within information science. By generalizing the separation of frequency and attribute, the Price equation uncovers elementary symmetries governing the fundamental geometric character of all transformation. The invariance of aggregate distribution, though elementary in isolation, imposes significant restrictions on how change can geometrically unfold. Combined with certain other constraints, this invariance accounts for why equations in disparate scientific domains display parallel structural patterns. text: This work presents Latent Relational Analysis (LRA), an approach designed to assess semantic resemblance. The core concept behind LRA involves evaluating the degree to which semantic connections between two word pairs correspond to one another. When such relational correspondence is sufficiently strong, the word pairs are considered analogous—as exemplified by cat:meow and dog:bark, which exhibit high relational overlap. Cognitive science research suggests that relational correspondence plays a central role in numerous cognitive and linguistic phenomena, including analogical reasoning. The traditional Vector Space Model (VSM) framework determines relational similarity by computing the cosine distance between vectors representing each word pair, where vector components are derived from frequencies of pre-specified patterns extracted from textual corpora. LRA advances this framework through three key innovations: (1) automatic extraction of patterns from the corpus rather than manual specification, (2) application of Singular Value Decomposition to regularize frequency information, and (3) incorporation of synonymy to rephrase word pairs. This article outlines the LRA computational procedure and provides empirical evaluation against VSM across two benchmarks: solving college entrance exam word analogy problems and categorizing semantic relations within noun-modifier pairs. LRA demonstrates superior performance, matching human competency levels on analogy tasks while substantially outperforming VSM across both evaluation metrics.

text:
Random Fourier features (RFF) have emerged as a widely adopted approach for approximating radial basis function (RBF) kernels, though they suffer from considerable variance. Remarkably, this variance can be significantly diminished through a straightforward normalization procedure, as we establish theoretically. We introduce the normalized RFF (NRFF) as an enhancement to the standard method.

Additionally, we introduce the generalized min-max (GMM) kernel as a similarity metric for data. The positive definiteness of GMM is guaranteed by an associated linearization technique called generalized consistent weighted sampling (GCWS), which converts this nonlinear kernel into a linear form. Our comprehensive empirical analysis spans over 50 publicly accessible datasets and compares the RBF and GMM kernels. The GMM kernel (which requires no hyperparameter tuning) surpasses even optimally-tuned RBF kernels across most of these datasets.

We perform comparative experiments on linearized versions of both kernels: RBF with NRFF and GMM with GCWS. Our findings demonstrate that GCWS achieves comparable classification performance while consuming substantially fewer samples than NRFF across most datasets, including cases where the original RBF kernel exhibits superior performance. This practical advantage of GCWS over NRFF is supported by theoretical analysis. On one hand, GCWS exhibits substantially lower relative variance (scaled by the squared mean) relative to NRFF in most similarity regimes, with the exception of very high similarity regions where both achieve near-zero variance. On the other hand, under reasonable data assumptions, we can prove analytically that GCWS produces markedly lower variance than NRFF when estimating comparable quantities (such as the RBF kernel), with the exception again being very high similarity regions.

When both parties understand these constraints beforehand, an entropy encoder can readily achieve near-optimal information transfer. However, our focus concerns scenarios where solely the transmitter possesses this knowledge—termed constrained coding in this context. The Kuznetsov-Tsybakov framework, addressing cases where only the transmitter recognizes which bits remain constant, represents a notable instance that unexpectedly achieves comparable efficiency to situations with mutual knowledge of the constraints. The task of discovering suitable encoding resembles solving analogous problems in error correction, yet rather than a single guaranteed solution, there exist multiple statistically probable alternatives. Unlike conventional steganography, which conceals data in minimally significant bits, our approach generates patterns matching the target image—embedding information within the flexibility of representing gray tones using black and white pixels via halftoning. Additional considerations include merging with error correction schemes and extensions toward rate distortion applications.

text: When practitioners engage in information extraction, raw datasets are typically organized into pattern matrices and then processed using statistical and linear algebraic techniques—principal component analysis being a prime example. The core issue stems from how linear algebra's matrix framework conceptualizes information movement as wave-like propagation, though in reality information sometimes behaves like particles, traveling along optimal routes. This computational and informational wave-particle duality was initially identified by Abramsky. This work explores an alternative particle-based conceptualization of information through the lens of *distance spaces*, mathematical structures that extend metric spaces while remaining somewhat more restrictive than Lawvere's *generalized metric spaces*. Through this perspective, locating the 'principal components' within a data matrix becomes equivalent to performing a bicompletion operation, drawing from enriched category theory. We elaborate on how the bicompletion process applies specifically to distance matrices. The underlying practical motivation driving this investigation is to devise techniques for assessing the computational difficulty of cryptographic attack strategies.

text:
While folksonomies offer a participatory approach to organizing content through user-generated tags that capture public sentiment, they lack the structure of formal classification schemes and present interpretability challenges. To enable meaningful collaborative and social tag alignment, users would need to operate within shared contextual frameworks. A potential remedy involves implementing a dynamic folksonomy framework that assists users in selecting appropriate tags and allows the system to adapt over time. This research employs cluster analysis to examine "Folksodriven," a novel framework incorporating three dimensions: tags, their sources, and temporal data. Various existing methodologies aim to leverage evolving folksonomies for feature assessment, yet this work takes a different approach by constructing a weighted tag network wherein edge weights reflect how frequently tags co-occur. The study then examines weight patterns and how node connectivity varies throughout the network. The paper presents and evaluates the network topology of Folksodriven tags—functioning as tag recommendations for users—using data collected from selected web platforms. The analysis reveals that Folksodriven hypergraphs demonstrate strong interconnectedness with comparatively short path distances between nodes, thereby enhancing users' capacity to stumble upon relevant and engaging material. Subsequently, the Clustering Coefficient measurement is benchmarked against those of random graphs. This research aims to provide valuable insights into folksonomy implementation across prominent, high-traffic websites with genuine user participation. The proposed folksonomy-based tagging approach presents a promising technique applicable to knowledge management systems.

*** This paper has been accepted to the International Conference on Social Computing and its Applications (SCA 2011) - Sydney Australia, 12-14 December 2011 ***

text: This article addresses and resolves five key misconceptions regarding how culture evolves, drawing on observations by Henrich, Boyd, and Richerson (2008). To begin with, cultural ideas should not be viewed as either wholly fixed or fluid entities; instead, they exist as patterns scattered throughout neural networks that react to specific details. Such distribution permits the formation of connections and allows for cultural transformation to occur. Second, the concept of 'replicator dynamics' fails to guarantee that natural selection operates. The replicator framework misses a crucial distinction: between actively decoded self-replicating information and passively transmitted self-referential material. This oversight obscures a core evolutionary principle—that genetic material passes to offspring, but traits gained during one's lifetime do not. Third, culture consistently violates this distinction through the prevalence of learned modifications. Furthermore, selective copying proves far less significant to cultural development than the inventive mechanisms through which new elements emerge. Fourth, no clear scientific standard exists for assessing what constitutes cultural success. Fifth, the role of chance variation warrants examination. The analysis ultimately argues that natural selection functions poorly as a conceptual tool for understanding cultural phenomena.

text: Black hole information paradox remains a deeply puzzling phenomenon in theoretical physics. Many researchers have assumed that Hawking's reasoning lacks mathematical rigor, and that incorporating subtle quantum corrections into the analysis would render the radiation emission process unitary. We demonstrate this assumption to be incorrect, establishing that minute perturbations to the primary Hawking calculation are unable to eliminate the correlation linking the emitted radiation to the original black hole. We present Hawking's reasoning as a formal `theorem': under the conditions of `standard' physics operating at the horizon boundary combined with customary locality requirements, physics must inevitably yield either mixed states or remnant particles. text:
Shannon developed a security framework assuming attackers possessed unbounded computational capability: *any information transmitted by a source would inevitably be obtained by Shannon's adversary*. Diffie and Hellman extended this threat model by acknowledging that practical adversaries face computational constraints. This development evolved into a transformative concept within computer science, ultimately giving rise to contemporary cryptography.

Shannon additionally addressed adversaries with boundless logical and observational abilities, encapsulated in the principle that "the adversary understands the system". Cryptography continues to embrace this perspective today. The widely accepted formulation, originating from Kerckhoffs's work, asserts that "obscuring the system provides no genuine protection", implying that encryption methods cannot remain hidden from adversaries and that security must depend exclusively on confidential keys. Contemporary cryptography actually surpasses both Shannon and Kerckhoffs by implicitly presuming that *should an algorithm exist capable of compromising the system, the adversary will inevitably discover it*. Rather than depicting the attacker as an all-powerful computing machine, modern theory portrays the attacker as an infinitely capable programmer.

However, the Diffie-Hellman transition from unlimited to constrained computational resources has not been paralleled by a transition from unlimited to constrained logical or programming resources. Does assuming that adversaries will eventually discover and deploy any workable algorithm fundamentally differ from presuming that all computable functions will ultimately be executed? This work investigates approaches to enhance current representations of attackers and defenders by recognizing their finite logical and programming capabilities. When an adaptive adversary continuously probes the system to identify weaknesses, might the system achieve additional robustness by dynamically acquiring knowledge of the adversary's tactics and modifying its defenses accordingly?
label: ai

Computability logic (CL) constitutes a rigorous formal system addressing computational processes and their associated costs. It functions, in certain respects, as a semantics-grounded counterpart to (syntactically defined) linear logic. Leveraging its rich and adaptable linguistic framework, wherein computational challenges are represented by formulas and "truth" denotes computational decidability, CL may serve as a robust logical foundation for constructively-oriented applied disciplines and technological platforms that demand logically sound constructive and computationally substantive underpinnings.

Heyting's intuitionistic calculus INT stands among the most prominent constructivist logics, and its expressive apparatus constitutes a subset of CL's linguistic resources. The constructivist orientation underlying INT, nevertheless, has lacked a genuinely satisfactory and rigorously formalized semantic foundation. CL holds considerable promise for furnishing precisely such a foundation and for realizing Kolmogorov's celebrated pronouncement that "INT = logic of problems". This work establishes a soundness result demonstrating that INT conforms to CL semantics. Detailed information regarding CL may be found at http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html

The structure underlying CL remains flexible and susceptible to growth through successive refinements as investigation progresses. To execute a sequential combination of games requires engaging each component successively, proceeding from one to the next.

The central technical contribution of this work establishes a reliable and comprehensive formal system for the propositional component of computability logic, where the operator vocabulary—alongside standard negation—comprises all three conjunction and disjunction variants: parallel, choice, and sequential. text:
Stone duality serves as a unifying mathematical tool that brings together several previously disconnected areas within Theoretical Computer Science: Domain Theory, which Scott developed as a mathematical foundation for denotational semantics and the theory of computation; the study of concurrent systems and behavioral properties, advanced by Milner and Hennessy through operational semantics; and program logics.

Stone duality establishes a bridge connecting semantics (understood as domains of points representing what computational processes denote) with logics (conceived as lattices encoding process properties). Crucially, the underlying logical structure is geometric in nature, which translates computationally into the logic of detectable properties—specifically, those characteristics that can be verified for a process using only finite observational data from its execution.

This conceptual foundation motivates the following approach:

1. A metalanguage is constructed containing
- types = domains of discourse suitable for describing various computational phenomena.
- terms = programs = syntactic representations of potential models or points.

2. The metalanguage receives a standard denotational interpretation, whereby types are assigned domains and terms are mapped to domain values.

3. Independently, the metalanguage acquires a *logical* interpretation, where types correspond to propositional theories and terms are explicated through a program logic that formally characterizes their defining properties.

4. These dual interpretations are connected by establishing their Stone duality relationship. This ensures that semantics and logic remain mutually consistent, with each actually determining the other up to isomorphic equivalence.

This framework enables numerous practical possibilities. Beginning with a denotational characterization of some computational phenomenon expressed in our metalanguage, one can mechanically derive the corresponding logic for analyzing that phenomenon.
label: ai

text: This work presents an enhancement to the sequent calculus framework known as cirquent calculus. Traditional Gentzen-style proof trees maintain disjoint formula sequences among related sequents, whereas cirquent calculus permits these sequents to have overlapping components. By enabling explicit control over whether resources may be distributed among multiple sequents—thereby bringing greater nuance to the resource-sensitive philosophy at the heart of substructural logics—cirquent calculus provides substantially greater adaptability and expressive power compared to its predecessor. The emergence of computability logic (http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html), a semantically grounded formal system studying computational phenomena, demanded innovative proof-theoretic machinery. This new domain had resisted formalization through conventional syntactic methods. Cirquent calculus successfully addresses this obstacle. When contraction is eliminated from the complete rule set, a sound and complete derivation system for computability logic's foundational subsystem CL5 emerges. The identical modification to sequent calculus, by contrast, undermines the system substantially, yielding the comparatively impoverished affine logic. Computability logic suggests that CL5, rather than affine logic, properly captures the resource-centered worldview previously attributed to the latter framework. To bolster this assertion, the present work develops an abstract semantics for resource behavior and establishes both soundness and completeness of CL5 relative to this semantics.

text: Computability logic (CL) (see http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html) represents an emerging initiative aimed at reconceptualizing logic as a mathematical framework centered on computability rather than truth—the traditional focus of logical inquiry. In this framework, computational tasks are represented by formulas, algorithmic solvability constitutes "truth," and proofs serve as encodings of such solutions. Building on previous research that has pursued increasingly powerful axiomatizations of CL fragments, this work proposes the novel deductive system CL12 and establishes both its soundness and completeness relative to CL's underlying semantics. By expanding classical predicate logic while maintaining conservative extensions and supplying substantially increased expressive capacity and deductive capability, CL12 offers a viable, computationally grounded, and constructive rival to classical logic for use in practical applications and theories. To exemplify this potential, the paper transforms the classical-logic-founded framework of Peano arithmetic into an equivalent system grounded in computability logic principles. A key objective of this work involves laying groundwork for what the author envisions as an emerging research direction with significant promise—namely, systematic investigation into the distinctive and likely quite singular metatheoretical properties characteristic of CL-based arithmetic systems and related CL-based formal theories.

text: The computational intricacy of context-free languages receives examination here, with emphasis placed on two recognized structural characteristics: immunity and pseudorandomness. Our findings include: (i) a context-free REG-immune language exists that falls outside REG/n, and (ii) a REG-bi-immune language can be recognized in deterministic logarithmic space. Additionally, (iii) a CFL-simple set exists, defined as an infinite context-free language whose negation maintains CFL-immunity. Drawing parallels with REG-immunity, REG-primeimmune languages lack polynomially dense regular subsets. Regarding pseudorandomness properties of context-free languages, we demonstrate (v) that REG/n-pseudorandom languages belong to CFL. text: Over the last thirty years, substantial advances have been achieved in analyzing combinatorial NP optimization problems alongside their corresponding optimization and approximation hierarchies, including NPO, PO, APX (or APXP), and PTAS. However, a group of problems residing within the P-solvable class PO has largely escaped rigorous scrutiny regarding their precise computational complexity. Addressing this gap requires extending and enhancing the current polynomial-time framework to enable deeper investigation of approximation algorithms applied to optimization problems in PO. We specifically examine problems defined through logarithmic-space and uniform-circuit computation models. Our work concentrates on nondeterministic logarithmic-space (NL) optimization or NPO problems. The investigation encompasses multiple optimization and approximation categories—namely NLO, LO, APXL, and LSAS—alongside newly introduced classes NC1O, APXNC1, NC1AS, and AC0O, which emerge from uniform Boolean circuit families. While many NL decision problems can be readily transformed into NL optimization (NLO) problems, extensive prior research on NLO problems remains limited. Consequently, we present several novel NLO problems belonging to these low-complexity categories. Leveraging NC1 or AC0 approximation-preserving reductions, we identify the hardest problems (termed complete problems) within each class. Additionally, we establish various hierarchical collapses and distinctions among these specialized optimization and approximation categories, both with and without relying on unresolved complexity assumptions.

text: Clarithmetics constitute frameworks within number theory grounded in computability logic (refer to http://www.csc.villanova.edu/~japaridz/CL/ ). Within these frameworks, formulas encode interactive computational problems, with "truth" defined as the availability of an algorithmic solution. Imposing different constraints on solution complexity yields distinct variants of clarithmetic. This work presents a parametric variant, CLA11(P1,P2,P3,P4), wherein adjusting three parameters P1, P2, P3 through systematic procedures automatically generates theories that are both sound and complete relative to numerous target tricomplexity classes—that is, combinations spanning temporal complexity (governed by P3), spatial complexity (governed by P2), and amplitude complexity (governed by P1). Soundness entails that each theorem T expresses an interactive number-theoretic computational problem possessing a solution within the specified tricomplexity class, and moreover, such solutions can be mechanically derived from T's proof. Completeness signifies that every interactive number-theoretic problem admitting a solution in the designated tricomplexity class corresponds to some system theorem. Additionally, by adjusting parameter P4, one can strengthen extensional completeness to intensional completeness—albeit forfeiting recursive axiomatizability while maintaining theoretical simplicity and elegance—whereby every formula describing a problem with a solution in the target tricomplexity class becomes a system theorem. The article appears in dual segments: the initial section presents the system and establishes completeness, whereas the subsequent section concentrates on establishing soundness.

We expand upon earlier work presented in Apt and Bezem [AB99] (referenced in cs.LO/9811017), which furnished an algorithmic interpretation of first-order expressions under any given interpretation. This paper builds on that foundation by establishing a denotational semantic framework for first-order logic. The framework draws together various established principles originating from both semantics of procedural programming and logic-based programming paradigms. Under this computational framework, the following correspondences emerge: conjunction functions as sequential execution, disjunction operates as ``don't know'' nondeterminism, existential quantification acts as introduction of a scoped variable, and negation implements the ``negation as finite failure'' strategy. A soundness theorem establishes that our semantic framework aligns with the standard interpretation of logical truth. text: In 1931, Kurt Goedel's groundbreaking work established what would become theoretical computer science while simultaneously laying out key ideas central to Artificial Intelligence (AI) theory. This maturation has enabled researchers to derive significant optimality findings for agents operating in physical worlds with incomplete information, achieved by merging Goedelian logical frameworks with probabilistic methods. This essay surveys pivotal developments throughout AI's past, highlights significant contemporary discoveries, and offers predictions regarding possibilities over the coming quarter-century. Special attention is paid to the transformative impact of accelerating computational power, as well as the potential of self-referential, recursively self-enhancing universal problem-solving systems grounded in Goedel's insights.
label: ai

text: The formal specification of games through computer science techniques presents an engaging challenge. This work provides a rigorous mathematical framework for two-player games grounded in ACP (Algebra of Communicating Process), a process algebra framework. We introduce a novel construct—the opponent's alternative composition operator (OA)—into ACP to represent both game trees and player strategies, resulting in what we call GameACP. A consistent and comprehensive set of axioms for this system is naturally derived. To capture game outcomes, which emerge from the combined actions of both the player and opponent, we incorporate an additional construct known as the playing operator (PO) within the GameACP framework to characterize process execution. For this operator too, we develop sound and complete axiomatizations. GameACP introduces certain non-deterministic behaviors that require further treatment; thus we adapt APTC, a truly concurrent process algebra, for game-theoretic applications, yielding GameAPTC. We conclude by establishing a correctness result that connects actual game outcomes with the formal derivations obtainable within both GameACP and GameAPTC systems.

text: Solomonoff synthesized Occam's razor with Epicurus' principle regarding multiple competing explanations into a single, mathematically rigorous, all-encompassing framework for inductive reasoning, thereby establishing algorithmic information theory as a discipline. This makes M suitable as a universal forecasting tool when mu remains unknown. The opening section examines whether computable universal (semi)measures exist and how they behave across different levels of computational complexity: primitive recursive, estimable, recursively enumerable, and approximable classes. For example, M is enumerable though not estimable, and it surpasses every other enumerable semimeasure. Demonstrations are supplied for both discrete and continuous settings. A broadened notion of randomness applicable to particular sequences is proposed and used to clarify complications in this domain. text: We present an axiomatization framework for quantum processes based on a quantum extension of the classical process algebra ACP (Algebra of Communicating Processes). Our approach utilizes quantum process configurations denoted $\langle p, \varrho\rangle$, which we decompose into two relatively autonomous components: the structural component $p$ and the quantum component $\varrho$. This decomposition proves essential since developing a sound and complete theory hinges critically on the structural characteristics of $p$. The quantum component $\varrho$ represents the execution outcomes of $p$, allowing us to study and verify the underlying principles of quantum mechanics. Beyond introducing a strong notion of bisimularity for quantum processes, we also develop a weak bisimularity concept that accounts for silent transitions and captures internal computations abstraction within quantum systems. We then investigate connections between quantum bisimularity and its classical counterpart, enabling the development of a rigorous axiomatization. The resulting system, designated qACP, integrates quantum information with classical information, thereby bridging quantum and classical computation frameworks. This unified approach proves practical and versatile for validating numerous quantum communication protocols.

text: We examine perspectives on whether rational understanding of the cosmos is achievable, beginning with Plato's philosophy, moving through Leibniz's contributions, and considering perspectives held by prominent twentieth-century researchers. Drawing on these historical and scientific foundations, we put forward the argument that understanding constitutes reduction—that is, accounting for numerous observations through a minimal set of basic principles—and that any explanatory framework functions as an algorithmic procedure generating empirical predictions. This reasoning motivates us to characterize something's degree of complexity as the magnitude of its most economical explanation, namely the shortest algorithm capable of producing it. This foundational principle underlies algorithmic information theory (AIT), an important domain within computational theory. Employing the mathematical tool of program-size complexity, we can identify mathematical truths that cannot be proven through any mathematical system less intricate than the facts themselves. This demonstrates that mathematical reality possesses boundless complexity and consequently resists complete understanding, at minimum in any fixed or unchanging sense. The question of whether physical reality exhibits bounded or unlimited complexity remains open. Mainstream scientific thought posits that physical systems incorporate stochasticity, implying infinite complexity as well, though the alternative scenario—a fully deterministic universe generating apparent randomness through deterministic methods—cannot be dismissed, particularly given current exploratory investigations by S. Wolfram.

text: One-way nondeterministic pushdown automata equipped with write-only output tapes generate multi-valued partial context-free language functions. This work addresses a central problem posed by Konstantinidis, Santean, and Yu [Act. Inform. 43 (2007) 395-417]: whether every multi-valued partial CFL function admits a single-valued partial CFL function as a refinement. The answer is negative. We demonstrate this by constructing a specific multi-valued partial CFL function and establishing that none of its refinements qualify as single-valued partial CFL functions. This finding stands in contrast to an earlier discovery by Kobayashi [Inform. Control 15 (1969) 95-109], who showed that multi-valued partial NFA functions—those realized by nondeterministic finite automata with output tapes—can invariably be refined to single-valued NFA functions. The counterexample presented here is unambiguously 2-valued, yielding a more refined separation: refinements of unambiguously 2-valued partial CFL functions cannot be single-valued. The proof employs a novel framework of colored automata, which lack output tapes but incorporate "colors" capable of emulating pushdown automata restricted to constant-space output. Using this construction, we perform detailed combinatorial investigations into the dynamics of transition sequences representing stack behavior (known as stack histories) within colored automata.

text: The measurement of complexity as a quantity has suffered from numerous conflicting and unclear formulations. Acknowledging the explicit presence of an observer—one who interprets and assigns significance to system data—provides a path through these contradictions. This perspective reveals that the many proposed complexity metrics represent different observer perspectives and/or approximate measures that correlate loosely with complexity while remaining computationally tractable given accessible information. Much of the field's conceptual disarray stems from science's conventional practice of excluding the observer from descriptions to preserve perceived objectivity. By openly recognizing the observer's role, clarity can extend to other perplexing domains. Emergence, another extensively debated phenomenon, becomes comprehensible when framed as an irreducibility gap separating description space from meaning space. Similarly, Quantum Mechanics emerges as fundamentally a theory about observation. Success in this area suggests a provocative possibility: that all physical phenomena might ultimately reduce to observer properties. This raises a critical question: what constitutes the essential (as distinct from incidental) characteristics of an observer? Addressing this demands a comprehensive theory of consciousness—a frontier still distant from resolution. Nevertheless, where progress has materialized, such as in Daniel Dennett's Consciousness Explained, a consistent element repeatedly surfaces: self-observation functions as an indispensable component.
