text: Self-delimiting (SLIM) programs represent a fundamental idea in computational theory, especially within the domains of algorithmic information and probability theory, as well as asymptotically optimal program search (AOPS). To leverage AOPS for (potentially recurrent) neural networks (NNs), this work proposes SLIM NNs. In a standard SLIM NN, individual neurons employ threshold-based activation mechanisms. As computation unfolds, signals propagate through the network from input neurons until reaching a designated halt neuron that terminates processing. The connection weights within the network encode its program. Programs that terminate constitute a prefix code structure. Resetting the network to its starting condition requires no greater computational expense than executing the most recent program. Given that early segments of SLIM programs shape their subsequent parts (weight modifications made initially during an episode determine which weights become relevant later), learning methods for SLIM NNs should apply weight modifications concurrently as signals spread. This becomes feasible by applying AOPS to expanding SLIM NNs. To efficiently train a SLIM NN across numerous tasks—such as categorizing diverse patterns or managing varied robotic control scenarios—every connection maintains a record of which tasks employ it. These records can be updated efficiently throughout the training process. When assessing how current weight modifications affect performance, SLIM NN learning methods need only reassess the straightforwardly determined collection of tasks that could be influenced by the modifications being tested. Planned implementations of SLIM NNs will utilize three-dimensional, brain-inspired parallel processing systems. Their associated learning methods will reduce task-dependent total connection length in active pathways, promoting efficient task decomposition through neuronal clusters positioned in close physical proximity. This emerging family of SLIM NN learning methods is presently undergoing investigation through experiments scheduled for separate publication.
label: ai

text: This work examines elastica through the lens of Riemannian geometry, addressing both one- and two-dimensional cases. A contrasting scenario involves the flexing of a planar two-dimensional surface where deformation is confined to horizontal directions while vertical elements remain essentially straight under small-strain conditions. Such configurations exhibit zero Gaussian curvature, though the Riemann curvature remains nonzero and correlates with the material's elastic characteristics. Non-Riemannian approaches prove valuable for analyzing rods featuring heterogeneous cross-sections, allowing Cartan torsion and the Riemann tensor to be formulated via the rod's twist. When cross-sections are uniform, the Riemann tensor can alternatively be derived from Kirchhoff's equations by contrasting them with non-geodesic motion and determining the affine connection. Applied loads on the rod function as the mechanism driving geodesic deviation within the space of combined Frenet curvature and twist parameters. The Riemann tensor itself can be expressed through the mechanical torque moment acting on the structure.

text:
This paper explores the remarkable and elegant interconnections emerging among several seemingly unrelated fields: Knot Theory, Categorical Quantum Mechanics, and Logic and Computation.

We concentrate particularly on two key areas:

- Historically, the Temperley-Lieb algebra has always been characterized as a quotient in some fashion: either through generators and relations following Jones' seminal work, or through a diagram algebra up to planar isotopy in Kauffman's formulation. Using methods from Geometry of Interaction—a dynamical understanding of proof reduction via Cut Elimination that grew out of Linear Logic—we provide a non-quotient characterization of the Temperley-Lieb category, what Computer Science calls a "fully abstract representation". This simultaneously enriches Geometry of Interaction by introducing a planar variant and demonstrating that the Cut Elimination interpretation (the "Execution Formula", or "feedback composition") maintains planarity.

- We additionally demonstrate that the Temperley-Lieb algebra offers a suitable framework for executing computation through diagram manipulation as topological simplification—"straightening tangled lines". For this end, we introduce a "planar lambda-calculus" and establish its compatibility with the Temperley-Lieb category.

text:
As internet technology expanded, the proliferation of digital information in public spaces prompted technology companies to focus increasingly on protecting data privacy. Currently, two primary approaches dominate the field: Cryptography and Steganography. Cryptography functions by transforming confidential content into unintelligible text, whereas steganography operates by concealing the message itself within a carrier medium such as a digital file. Within audio steganography specifically, this carrier takes the form of a digital sound file where confidential information is embedded, typically within the individual bits comprising audio samples.

This work introduces an innovative approach to embedding secret information within uncompressed sound files, employing both a probabilistic algorithm and a production system paired with a word database. The technique leverages two distinct carriers for transmitting concealed information between sender and receiver: An audio file serves as the first carrier, with randomly chosen audio samples functioning as the storage location for hidden information; simultaneously, the second carrier consists of syntactically valid English language text produced during execution via a production system, which indicates the positions of the selected random samples within the audio file. This methodology offers substantial resistance to discovery and extraction, making it challenging for adversaries to identify or access the embedded secret information. Testing results demonstrate the effectiveness of both the hiding and retrieval phases of this approach. Looking ahead, incorporation of semantic analysis is planned to enhance the generated intermediate text, ensuring it maintains not only syntactic validity but also semantic coherence.

text:
Aumann's influential 1976 result demonstrates that when rational Bayesian agents share identical prior beliefs, they cannot persistently maintain disagreement: once their views on any matter become mutually recognized, they must converge to the same opinion. While economists have extensively investigated the conditions required for this theorem, two fundamental issues remain unexplored: whether agents can actually achieve consensus through a conversation of practical duration, and whether the required computational steps can be executed efficiently. This work provides affirmative responses to both, thus reinforcing Aumann's foundational insight.

We establish that two agents operating from a shared prior can reach agreement within epsilon regarding the expected value of a [0,1] variable—with high probability relative to their prior—by transmitting merely order 1/epsilon^2 bits. Remarkably, this communication requirement exhibits no dependence whatsoever on n, the number of bits representing the agents' private information. The analysis then generalizes to settings with multiple agents; additionally, we present a case study demonstrating that the conventional approach used by economists (iteratively disclosing current expectations) nearly achieves this theoretical limit, whereas an alternative "attenuated protocol" performs superior. Furthermore, we propose a method allowing two Bayesian reasoners to achieve epsilon-agreement by exchanging order 1/epsilon^2 messages, implementable by computationally bounded agents. Specifically, external observers examining the agents' information and communication record would find no detectable difference from idealized Bayesians. The computational cost of this implementation grows exponentially with 1/epsilon^6, yet remains independent of n.
label: ai

text:
We introduce asymmetric stable random projections as a technique for estimating the pth frequency moments in evolving data streams (0<p<=2), a problem of considerable interest within theoretical computer science and database research. Our approach delivers substantial improvements—or even unbounded gains as p approaches 1—compared to existing techniques utilizing (symmetric) stable random projections.

The proposed technique accommodates data streams under three conditions: (a) containing only insertions (the cash-register model); (b) maintaining non-negative values throughout (the strict Turnstile model); or (c) becoming non-negative at designated checkpoints. These constraints represent minimal practical limitations for real-world applications.

Our technique demonstrates particularly strong performance when p = 1+/- \Delta with small \Delta values, addressing a practically relevant use case. In contexts like decay rates or interest rates, \Delta typically remains modest. When \Delta = 0, computing the first frequency moment (the aggregate sum) becomes nearly error-free through straightforward counting. Our approach functions as an enhanced counting mechanism capable of projecting future total values while incorporating effects from decay or accruing interest.

Our work presents two principal contributions. (A) We present the inaugural application of asymmetric stable random projections for this purpose. (B) Building from fundamental principles, we construct multiple statistical estimators tailored to asymmetric stable distributions, establishing their variance calculations, error and probability bounds, and consequently deriving associated sample complexity requirements.

text:
Spinors are defined through the framework of Clifford algebras. The connection between these algebraic structures and quantum information has both theoretical and practical foundations. When spin-1/2 systems serve as the underlying model for quantum bits, this creates a physically motivated link. Additionally, the mathematical elegance of these algebras provides further justification for such usage.

A key mathematical advantage lies in the fact that any complex Clifford algebra generated by 2n elements, denoted Cl(2n,C), can be represented as the full algebra of 2^n × 2^n complex matrices. This representation naturally incorporates all unitary matrices required for quantum n-qubit gates. Each element within this algebra maps to a general linear complex transformation. This becomes particularly valuable since linear operators used to describe restrictions of unitary operators to subspaces need not themselves be unitary.

A second significant property emerges from the elegant algebraic structure of Cl(2n), which decomposes naturally via tensor products of fundamental components. This decomposition parallels the compositional structure inherent in multi-qubit quantum systems. The efficient encoding provided by 2n generators proves especially practical for computational applications, enabling conventional computers to simulate elementary quantum circuits through modern software implementations.

text: Steganography represents a method for concealing sensitive information by embedding it within a digital carrier file while maintaining the file's integrity and dimensions. A key distinction exists between steganography and cryptography: steganography operates as a covert communication channel known exclusively to intended recipients, whereas cryptography functions as an open communication method recognizable to all observers, though its content remains encoded. An effective steganographic algorithm typically resists detection and prevents adversaries from understanding its operational mechanisms or extracting hidden data from the carrier medium. A common strategy for achieving this resistance involves applying digital modifications to the carrier file following the data embedding phase. Such modifications, however, are generally destructive, eliminating the concealed information in the process. The approach works by modulating the carrier image's luminosity after embedding confidential material within it. The proposed technique utilizes a configurable framework comprising three adjustable parameters that communicating parties can customize: the degree of brightness modification applied to the carrier image post-embedding, which color channels undergo luminosity adjustment, and which bytes will contain the hidden message. The method's key innovation lies in encoding secret data bits into three least significant bits of the carrier image's constituent bytes in a manner that preserves the encrypted content when the carrier image's original brightness is subsequently restored. Testing and analysis validated that the suggested approach functions effectively and reliably.

text: This work examines how particles escape from a metastable cubic potential well using quantum mechanical techniques, specifically employing the Euclidean path integral formalism within the semiclassical framework. As energy increases toward the sphaleron configuration, the oscillation period changes smoothly and monotonically, indicating a continuous transition between purely quantum and thermally activated processes. The suppression of quantum fluctuations around these paths is determined exactly using functional determinant methods and evaluated throughout the low-temperature regime extending to this crossover point. Particular attention is given to the single negative mode of the fluctuation operator, which contributes an imaginary component to the partition function; this mode is characterized by solving the Lamé differential equation. For particles with large inertia, the escape rate exhibits pronounced temperature sensitivity driven primarily by a soft zero-frequency mode; near the crossover region, it surpasses zero-temperature predictions by approximately five-fold. Beyond this maximum, classical Arrhenius kinetics becomes dominant. Investigation of the quartic variant reveals analogous behavior, though particles persist roughly ten times longer in this potential compared to the cubic case under equivalent conditions. Connections to stochastic activation phenomena in classically bistable systems are examined.

text: What sets humans apart from other animals may well be our capacity for creative thinking. This involves the ability to move fluidly between two distinct cognitive modes, each suited to particular challenges. One mode, known as divergent thinking, enables us to conceive of numerous possible answers to a problem. The other, convergent thinking, allows us to identify the single correct response. Unconventional viewpoints characterize divergent thinking, whereas convergent thinking relies on more traditional angles of approach. Individuals with creative abilities tend to demonstrate openness to novel experiences, comfort with uncertainty, and strong self-assurance. Research exploring connections between creativity and mood disorders presents conflicting findings. Brain imaging studies using fMRI and EEG indicate that creative processes correlate with reduced cognitive constraint and lower levels of mental activation. The brain's associative networks, organized in a distributed and content-addressable manner, naturally facilitate retrieval of pertinent information without requiring deliberate effort. Creative expression emerged in human civilization at least three million years ago through primitive stone implements, and the Paleolithic era witnessed the simultaneous emergence of artistic, scientific, and spiritual endeavors. Debate persists regarding how much creative achievement depends on skill development, lucky breaks, or instinctive knowing; whether the creative act itself or its final result matters most; whether creative capacity transfers across different domains or remains isolated to specific fields; how strongly creativity correlates with mental health conditions; and whether divergent thinking generates multiple separate concepts or progressively refines one initially unclear idea that yields various observable manifestations. Future investigations should focus on computational approaches, understanding creativity's neurobiological foundations, and longitudinal examination of how ideas develop over extended periods.

text: The replicator framework developed by Dawkins has prompted misuse of selectionist thinking throughout social science fields, primarily because it fails to resolve the central issue that motivated the original theory of natural selection: in what manner do living things build upon modifications when characteristics developed throughout life are eliminated? Von Neumann's self-replicating automaton (SRA) concept provides a solution to this problem. An SRA operates through a self-assembly code that functions in two separate capacities: (1) interpreted dynamically during growth to produce a nearly identical duplicate, and (2) mechanically transferred to the duplicate to guarantee reproductive capability. Traits picked up during an organism's existence do not pass to descendants, but traits obtained through the copying procedure do transmit forward. Cultural systems differ fundamentally in that they retain acquired modifications indefinitely. Acquired modifications can build exponentially quicker than inherited ones and ultimately dominate them as different versions replicate at unequal rates under selective forces. Such dynamics make selectionism unsuitable but leave evolutionary approaches open for understanding culture. Contemporary investigations into how life originated point to an older, less orderly evolutionary mechanism termed communal exchange, which operates without a self-assembly code and preceded the emergence of natural selection. It is argued that communal exchange offers a superior evolutionary model for cultural development in place of selectionism. Computational simulations of how culture evolves and network-based systems for tracking the evolution of material goods support this argument, and the framework aligns with documented patterns of extensive human collaborative behavior.

text: During the 1860s, Clausius put forth a thermodynamic quantity to which he gave the designation {\it entropy} $S$. This pivotal thermodynamic property was characterized as {\it extensive}, meaning that in modern language, $S(N) \propto N$ holds as $N \to\infty$ in the thermodynamic limit. Around ten years later, Boltzmann formulated a mathematical expression for this quantity that relates $S$ to the likelihood of observing particular microscopic states (termed {\it complexions} in that era) within the system. In contemporary language, for a system containing $W$ achievable discrete states, this mathematical relation takes the form $S_{BG}=-k_B \sum_{i=1}^W p_i \ln p_i$. A defining characteristic of BG entropy is its {\it additivity}: when systems A and B are statistically independent, we have $S_{BG}(A+B)=S_{BG}(A)+S_{BG}(B)$. For more than a century, the concepts {\it extensive} and {\it additive} were largely treated interchangeably, while $S_{BG}$ stood as the sole conceivable mathematical form for $S$. The formula $S_{BG}$ was thus deemed {\it universal}. Contemporary understanding demonstrates that this assumption is {\it incorrect}; these terms are {\it not} equivalent but merely overlap when considering standard Hamiltonians with {\it short-range} interactions among constituents, lacking severe frustration and similar anomalies. These observations underpin a reformulation of BG entropy and statistical mechanics introduced in 1988, commonly designated nonadditive entropy $S_q$ and nonextensive statistical mechanics. This review examines these considerations and illustrates recent and representative uses of such frameworks across biological, engineered, and societal contexts, substantiated through mathematical analysis, laboratory testing, empirical data, and numerical simulations.
label: ai

text: The practice of embedding concealed digital information within other data such that its presence remains undetected is known as steganography. While cryptography can draw attention through its use of encryption, steganography operates covertly, permitting secretive data exchange between parties. The Canny algorithm itself depends on three adjustable parameters: a Gaussian filter kernel size, a lower threshold, and an upper threshold. By varying these parameters, the same image and secret data can produce entirely different outputs, creating significant uncertainty around the algorithm's operation and making it substantially harder for attackers to pinpoint where hidden content resides. Testing was conducted using GhostBit, a tool designed to both encode and decode concealed information through the proposed method. text:
Cryptography and steganography represent two distinct approaches to ensuring secure transmission of digital information. While both serve protective purposes, they operate on fundamentally different principles. Cryptography functions by rendering data incomprehensible through scrambling techniques, thereby preventing unauthorized access by potential interceptors. Steganography, by contrast, operates by concealing the presence of data itself, enabling transmission without drawing attention. Specifically, steganography embeds confidential information—such as text—within another medium, like an image.

Although steganography encompasses numerous existing variants, no established method currently applies this approach to database query languages like SQL. This work introduces an innovative steganography technique designed for text-based information. The methodology encodes secret messages into SQL carriers constructed from SELECT statements. The resulting SQL carrier emerges dynamically from the secret message through a word dictionary structured as a hash table containing 65 distinct categories, each corresponding to a linguistic character. For each character requiring concealment, a random word from the appropriate category gets selected. Upon processing all characters, these words combine to construct a functional SQL query.

Practical experiments substantiated the viability of this methodology, validating its theoretical foundations. Forthcoming investigations will extend this framework to encompass additional SQL statement types—specifically INSERT, DELETE, and UPDATE operations—thereby increasing the complexity and resilience of SQL carriers against attempts by unauthorized parties to extract embedded secret messages.

This work addresses an inverse problem associated with the standard wave equation posed in an unbounded domain. Mathematically, it reformulates an inverse obstacle problem that utilizes time-dependent scattering information generated by acoustic waves measured across a bounded temporal window. The wave at the obstacle's surface is governed by a Robin-type boundary condition featuring an unknown spatially-dependent parameter.

The acoustic disturbance originates from initial conditions concentrated away from the obstacle and is recorded during a finite time span at the location where the initial data is supported. Previous research has established that the enclosure method permits identification of the largest ball whose complement contains the obstacle, recoverable from the measurement data.

Furthermore, an important novel finding shows: the enclosure method remains applicable when observations are collected on a spherical surface centered at the support of the initial perturbation, producing analogous conclusions for items (i) and (ii).

text:
The concealment of confidential messages so that only the intended parties recognize their existence is known as steganography. Distinct from cryptography, which merely disguises secret information while keeping it visible, steganography embeds covert data within carrier files like images and transmits them with complete discretion, thereby minimizing the risk of detection by interceptors. Nevertheless, since public communication channels are subject to surveillance by eavesdroppers, they remain susceptible to stego-attacks—attempts to forcibly extract hidden information from the carrier medium by trial and error.

This vulnerability often stems from steganalysts' typical assumption that hidden information resides in a sole medium file rather than across several complementary files. This work introduces a text steganography technique designed to conceal textual secrets using dual carriers: a Pangram (a sentence incorporating every letter of the alphabet) and an uncompressed picture file. The technique operates by locating each character from the secret message within the Pangram text, commencing from a randomly chosen position referred to as the seed and concluding at the position of the character's initial appearance. This yields two position values: the seed index and the offset index. These indices are subsequently encoded into the bottom three bits of the image's color channel values. Following encoding, both the Pangram and image are delivered to the recipient.

The primary benefit of this strategy lies in its enhanced security—by distributing secret information across two distinct carriers instead of a single one, unauthorized recovery becomes significantly more difficult. To demonstrate its practical application, experiments were performed illustrating both the encryption and decryption processes for hidden text utilizing the Pangram and image mediums.

We introduce the concept of a well-clusterable dataset by merging two perspectives: the $k$-means clustering algorithm's goal (reducing the spread of points around centroids) and intuitive reasoning (clusters should be separated by empty space). text:
When searching molecular databases for novel pharmaceuticals, researchers frequently need to identify compounds with comparable molecular geometries, as structural similarity often correlates with analogous biological effects. The immense scale of contemporary databases necessitates efficient preliminary screening techniques, many of which rely on feature vectors designed so that structurally similar molecules map to proximate vector values. Ultrafast Shape Recognition (USR) exemplifies this methodology, employing 12-dimensional vectors derived from the first three moments of distances calculated from four designated reference points. However, such vectors often contain redundant correlations and cannot be used to restore the underlying shape representation. By contrast, spherical harmonic (SH) decomposition operates with orthogonal coordinates, implying mutual independence and consequently greater information density within the feature vector. Customarily, rotationally invariant SH descriptors are utilized, yet this approach discards potentially crucial structural information.

This work presents a framework for descriptors incorporating controlled rotation normalization techniques, such as principal component analysis applied to spherical harmonics (PCA-SH). Given that particularly important ligand candidates must fit into protein binding sites, we present descriptors specifically tailored for thin, elongated molecular geometries. The bent deformed cylinder (BDC) model represents molecules as cylinders that undergo bending followed by deformation, transforming cross-sections into ellipses with changing properties. Legendre polynomials characterize the central axis of the curved cylinder, while supplementary polynomials encode how the elliptical cross-sections evolve along this axis. We also examine bent cylindrical harmonics (BCH), which substitutes elliptical cross-sections with cylindrical harmonic descriptions. These rotation-normalized descriptors enable reconstruction of approximate shape information, making them suitable for applications requiring lossy compression as well.
label: ai

text: At present, computation is understood as a mechanism that advances (or delays) a fully specified initial state vector into its corresponding terminal state vector. The initial and terminal state vectors respectively embody the (logical) inputs and outputs of a reversible Boolean network, while forward propagation represents a unidirectional spread of logical consequence moving from input toward output. In contrast, difficult NP-complete problems feature bidirectional propagation of logical consequence, flowing both from input to output and in the reverse direction, because both directions begin with partial information already established. Within a computational framework, logical consequence can be distributed in both forward and backward directions by constructing the appropriate gate configuration for the entire reversible Boolean network and simultaneously constraining both the output and input bit values. This work examines whether the physical behavior of such a network can be characterized through a propagation framework employing both delayed and advanced temporal components. text: This article introduces an innovative theoretical framework for understanding chemical equilibrium through Discrete Thermodynamics of Chemical Equilibria (DTd). The DTd framework conceptualizes chemical equilibrium as an equilibrium between internal and external thermodynamic forces (TdF) exerting influence on a chemical system. At the heart of DTd lies a logistic map describing the chemical system's thermodynamic states, which integrates the energetic features of chemical reactions transpiring in the system, the degree to which the system deviates from "genuine" thermodynamic equilibrium (TdE), and the external thermodynamic forces responsible for such deviation. The map's solutions yield pitchfork bifurcation diagrams plotted using "deviation from TdE" and "growth factor (or TdF)" as coordinates; the branches of these diagrams contain points representing various thermodynamic states of the system. These diagrams identify three distinct regimes: genuine thermodynamic equilibrium and open equilibrium along the thermodynamic branch preceding the stability threshold (i.e., the bifurcation point), and a bifurcation region exhibiting bistability and chaotic fluctuations beyond this threshold. The collection of solutions delineates the chemical system's state domain. This new theoretical approach satisfies the correspondence principle: when a chemical system is isolated, external TdF become negligible, and the fundamental map reduces to the conventional formulation of chemical equilibrium expressed through thermodynamic affinity. The framework unifies both established and modern thermodynamic theories of chemical equilibrium under a single coherent foundation. This article constitutes a substantially reorganized and reoriented treatment of earlier preliminary work on DTd fundamentals, enriched with additional findings.

text:
First-order methods currently dominate stochastic gradient descent applications in neural network training, yet they neglect information about local curvature relative to the optimum. While second-order methods provide the curvature data necessary for computing ideal step sizes, they face substantial practical obstacles, most notably that the full Hessian matrix scales quadratically with the number of parameters.

This work presents a modest extension of momentum-based first-order methods toward second-order optimization: fitting a parabolic model along a single coordinate direction—specifically the normalized momentum vector $\hat{v}$. The approach works by identifying a linear relationship in gradient behavior $\vec{g}=\nabla F(\vec{\theta})$ along the $\hat{v}$ axis, approximating $g(\vec{\theta}_\bot+\theta\hat{v})\approx \lambda (\theta -p)$ where $\theta = \vec{\theta}\cdot \hat{v}$, $g= \vec{g}\cdot \hat{v}$, and $\vec{\theta}_\bot=\vec{\theta}-\theta\hat{v}$. Through linear regression, parameters $\lambda$ and $p$ are computed via MSE minimization by maintaining just four running statistics ($g$, $\theta$, $g\theta$, $\theta^2$) in the selected direction. Exponential moving averages enable efficient online updates while progressively downweighting earlier gradient information. By monitoring the sign of curvature $\lambda$, the method can avoid saddle points—unlike standard Newton approaches that attract to them. For remaining coordinate directions outside the second-order model, conventional techniques like gradient descent apply simultaneously.

Additionally, a learning rate formula $\mu=\sigma_\theta / \sigma_g$ is derived, enabling adaptive variants of SGD where each parameter receives its own (second-order) optimized learning rate.
label: ai

text:
A central challenge in artificial life research involves developing systems capable of unbounded evolutionary growth. This work investigates two significant variables affecting this challenge through experiments conducted within the Tierra platform.

One key variable is {\em complexity penalty}, which discourages the evolution of intricate life forms by imposing reproduction costs proportional to genome length, thereby biasing the system toward streamlined designs. Within Tierra, the \verb+SlicePow+ parameter governs this penalty mechanism. When maximum penalty is applied, evolutionary processes drive the ancestral strain toward minimal simplicity. Conversely, when penalty is minimized, organisms tend to expand in size without becoming genuinely more sophisticated, accumulating non-functional genetic material instead. This investigation explores varying \verb+SlicePow+ settings between 0.9 and 1.0 to identify whether an optimal configuration exists for fostering increased organizational sophistication.

Natural biological systems differ fundamentally, drawing on abundant genuine randomness sources ultimately rooted in quantum phenomena, thereby circumventing such complexity restrictions. Computer systems can access {\em entropy harvesters}—specialized tools that supply legitimate random data—and this research documents how substituting Tierra's pseudo random generator with an entropy harvester influences system behavior.

text: This work builds upon findings presented in two preceding studies. Earlier work [J. Schubert, On nonspecific evidence, Int. J. Intell. Syst. 8 (1993) 711-725] introduced the metaconflict function as a tool within Dempster-Shafer theory, enabling the division of multiple evidentiary items into groups where the associated propositions lack precise specification—that is, where uncertainty exists regarding which particular event a given proposition addresses. A subsequent investigation [J. Schubert, Specifying nonspecific evidence, in Cluster-based specification techniques in Dempster-Shafer theory for an evidential intelligence analysis of multiple target tracks, Ph.D. Thesis, TRITA-NA-9410, Royal Institute of Technology, Stockholm, 1994, ISBN 91-7170-801-4] advanced this work by identifying not only the most probable grouping for each piece of evidence but also calculating the likelihood that any given piece of evidence belongs to each potential grouping. The present study pursues a different objective: determining a posterior probability distribution over the quantity of subsets. The approach rests on the principle that evidence within any subset bolsters the subset's existence to an extent proportional to the overall evidentiary strength of that evidence. Through this reasoning, a basic probability assignment can be constructed that addresses the determination of subset quantity. This assignment may subsequently be merged with a pre-established prior distribution over the domain to yield the desired posterior distribution.

text:
An earlier study [J. Schubert, On nonspecific evidence, Int. J. Intell.
Syst. 8(6), 711-725 (1993)] introduced the metaconflict function as a criterion
within Dempster-Shafer theory. This function enables the division of multiple
evidence items into subsets when their propositions lack specificity—that is,
when uncertainty exists regarding which event a given proposition addresses.
Each resulting subset corresponds to a distinct event. The metaconflict function
was conceptualized as the likelihood that the proposed partitioning holds true,
treating the conflict arising from Dempster's rule within each subset as a
newly generated metalevel piece of evidence whose proposition argues against the
entire partitioning scheme. The current work builds upon these earlier findings.
Rather than merely identifying the most probable subset for each evidence item,
as the previous work accomplished, we now go further. We determine the degree of
specificity for each nonspecific piece of evidence by calculating the likelihood
that it belongs to each possible subset, thereby revealing to which events the
proposition may pertain. As a byproduct, this approach identifies potentially
unreliable evidence. We subsequently introduce a novel framework for utilizing
these newly clarified evidence items in further analysis. This framework
encompasses techniques for adjusting the weight of evidence based on both
falsity likelihood and credibility stemming from incomplete specification of
affiliation, along with an enhanced procedure for determining each subset's
corresponding event.
label: ai

text: EVOC (standing for EVOlution of Culture) represents a computational framework that permits exploration of how different conditions—such as obstacles to cultural transmission, the existence and determination of influential figures, or shifts in the balance between novel creation and replication—shape both the richness and success of concept systems. The model employs agents built on neural network architecture that both generate novel ideas for behaviors and adopt the behaviors of neighboring agents. Its theoretical foundation posits that cultural change does not involve the propagation of memes or physical objects, but rather the underlying cognitive representations that produce them, advancing through a Lamarckian mechanism centered on the interchange of creative processes rather than through Darwinian competitive elimination. Within EVOC, the typical effectiveness of behaviors improves progressively, while behavioral variety initially expands before eventually contracting. The degree of behavioral variation correlates positively with both group scale and concentration, as well as with divisions separating different groups. Gradually softening boundaries between populations enhance behavioral effectiveness while sustaining variation by promoting the emergence of specialized practices followed by dissemination of superior behaviors. When a dominant figure broadcasts its behavioral patterns across the entire group, behavioral effectiveness rises but variation diminishes. Multiplying the quantity of influential figures lessens this outcome. Current investigations are examining scenarios in which an individual migrating between cultural contexts introduces innovative concepts while maintaining cultural compatibility.

text: Consider a scenario in which several domain experts or machine learning systems each propose distinct Bayesian network (BN) architectures. Our goal is to synthesize these proposals into a unified consensus BN architecture. The desired consensus structure should capture only those conditional independences that all proposed structures share while minimizing the quantity of associated model parameters. We demonstrate in this work that multiple non-isomorphic consensus BN structures can coexist and that computing any such structure constitutes an NP-hard problem. Given this computational difficulty, we turn to heuristic approaches as a practical alternative for obtaining approximate consensus BN architectures. This paper examines a particular heuristic strategy previously introduced in the literature, which relies on two core computational procedures—denoted Methods A and B—designed to efficiently compute the minimal directed independence map for a given BN structure under a specified node ordering. While these methods were presented as sound procedures, their correctness was never rigorously established; only informal justifications were provided. Our contribution demonstrates that both Methods A and B contain logical errors and presents corrected versions of these algorithms.

text: When equipped with suitable encodings of semantic associations—such as vector representations within a vector space framework—an appropriately designed algorithm can detect that the bond between carpenter and wood mirrors that between mason and stone (carpenter relates to wood just as mason relates to stone; these associations are parallel). Similarly, given encodings for dog, house, and kennel, an algorithm should identify that dog combined with house, forming the compound dog house, exhibits striking semantic resemblance to kennel (these two terms convey equivalent meaning). These two capabilities—detecting associations and modeling combinations—appear fundamentally intertwined. Nevertheless, existing leading approaches for associations diverge substantially from those excelling at combinations. We propose here a unified framework employing dual spaces that addresses both phenomena. This framework matches or exceeds the capabilities of preceding methods for both relations and combinations. The dual-space architecture incorporates one space for evaluating thematic kinship and another for gauging functional kinship. Carpenter and wood inhabit the carpentry domain; mason and stone inhabit the masonry domain. Carpenter and mason both embody the artisan function, whereas wood and stone both embody the material function. Within the compound dog house, kennel demonstrates thematic connections to both constituents (touching upon the pet and building domains). Kennel's functional character aligns with house's functional character (both serve as dwellings). By integrating thematic and functional resemblances through diverse combinations, one can capture associations, combinations, and broader semantic phenomena.

text: A standard Gaussian probability distribution representable through an AMP chain graph can be reformulated as a collection of linear relationships featuring correlated residuals, where the correlation pattern is determined by the chain graph's structure. Yet the chain graph encodes these residuals in an implicit manner, since no vertices within the chain graph serve to denote the residuals themselves. This paper proposes incorporating additional deterministic vertices into the chain graph to make the residuals explicit. The resulting structure is termed an EAMP chain graph. We demonstrate that each AMP chain graph maintains Markov equivalence with its corresponding EAMP chain graph once the error vertices are marginalized out. Furthermore, we establish that every EAMP chain graph, following marginalization of error vertices, is Markov equivalent to some LWF chain graph under similar marginalization, which in turn is Markov equivalent to a directed acyclic graph under marginalization of error vertices and conditioning on certain selection vertices. This finding is significant because it reveals that the independence relationships encoded by an AMP chain graph can be explained through some underlying data-generating mechanism that is incompletely observed and subject to selection effects. We additionally prove that EAMP chain graphs remain closed under marginalization, a valuable property ensuring that marginalized models maintain computational efficiency and simplicity.
label: ai

text: This study introduces a novel approach for characterizing pattern sequences by establishing a ceiling value around which the aggregate measurement can fluctuate in sync with the outputs generated from individual patterns. Each time a node activates, it simultaneously acquires an activation/deactivation control signal. When a node becomes active, it transmits a control response calibrated to match the incoming signal's magnitude. A stronger or positive incoming signal permits the node to retain an activation control for the subsequent cycle. Conversely, a weaker or negative incoming signal allows it to retain a deactivation control for the subsequent cycle. When a node remains inactive, the feedback state remains unaffected and the node adopts the control signal from the most recent activation event of that same neuron. The ceiling value consequently designates the broadest or most comprehensive pattern collection, whereas the floor value corresponds to the genuine set of active patterns. When a pattern sequence recurs cyclically, the measurement oscillates between these two boundaries, facilitating improved identification and quantification across successive intervals. Experimental results demonstrate that altering the sequence arrangement yields distinct measurement collections that are similarly quantifiable.

text: Consider the Winograd Schema (WS) — exemplified by sentences such as "The city councilmen refused the demonstrators a permit because they feared violence" alongside "The city councilmen refused the demonstrators a permit because they advocated revolution." This paper explores how the SP System, detailed in supplementary material, might address this interpretive challenge. The fundamental premise involves leveraging knowledge of non-contiguous patterns in linguistic features and the capacity to identify such recurring associations as a dependable approach to resolving pronoun references like "they." To tackle problems of this nature, any AI system requires suitable comprehension of pertinent grammatical and semantic information, which preferably would be self-acquired. Though the SP System demonstrates certain proficiencies in learning without supervision, its present performance in this domain remains insufficient to autonomously acquire the understanding necessary for WS interpretation, necessitating that such information be provided initially. Nonetheless, its demonstrated strengths in unsupervised learning indicate encouraging prospects for eventually acquiring the requisite knowledge to parse WS examples. Specifically, it shows promise in learning the aforementioned non-contiguous patterns characterizing linguistic feature associations.

S_q(A)/k+S_q(B)/k$. text: Using analytical methods, we determine how certain measurable quantities Y change over extended periods—specifically, averaged across a single complete orbit—when classical and general relativistic perturbations modify the standard two-body Newtonian dynamics for transiting exoplanets following elliptical paths. The particular observables we examine include eclipse duration, stellar radial velocity variations, and the time gap separating primary and secondary transit events. We investigate several dynamical mechanisms: rotational flattening in both the star and planet, mutual tidal deformations they induce on one another, gravitational influence from a nearby third body X, and relativistic effects from general relativity (encompassing both the Schwarzschild metric and frame-dragging). Our treatment systematically and uniformly incorporates how all relevant Keplerian orbit parameters respond to these perturbations. The methodology involves calculating instantaneous changes to each orbital element triggered by the considered dynamical effects, substituting these into the general formula for Y's instantaneous variation, and then averaging the resulting instantaneous rate $\dot Y(t)$ across one complete orbital cycle. This approach differs from commonly published treatments, which often employ simplified hybrid formulations that merely insert the secular precession of features like periastron directly into instantaneous expressions. We provide numerical results for a representative star-planet system and assess them against realistic observational precision achievable within a 10-year timeframe. While calibrated for this specific scenario, our findings extend to other astronomical situations and could facilitate tests of gravitational theories using bodies in our solar system, whether natural or artificial. (Abridged)

text: Recent work by Dvali and Vikman proposes that the faster-than-light neutrino observations from OPERA might originate from a terrestrial environmental effect. This effect would produce a long-range fifth force of gravitational nature, interacting with neutrinos through a coupling scale denoted M_*. The characteristic range lambda of this interaction must exceed one Earth radius R_e but cannot extend much beyond the Earth-Moon separation (approximately 60 R_e). We present an analytical treatment of how such a Yukawa-type fifth force affects orbital dynamics for objects orbiting a central mass. Our analysis applies broadly without depending on the specific value of lambda and encompasses all possible orbital configurations regardless of eccentricity e. The coupling strength parameter alpha receives stringent constraints from satellite laser ranging observations of LAGEOS II: |alpha| <= 1 10^-10-4 10^-9 for wavelengths between 1 and 10 R_e, implying M_* >= 4 10^9 -1.6 10^10. Lunar orbital precession provides even tighter limits of |alpha| <= 3 10^-11 for the Earth-Moon system when 15 R_e <= lambda <= 60 R_e, corresponding to M_* >= 3 10^10 - 4.5 10^10. However, these findings transcend both the OPERA superluminal framework and the specific Dvali-Vikman proposal (which predicts M_* = 10^-6 at lambda = 1 R_e, contrary to our constraints). Instead, they furnish general limits applicable to any theoretical framework incorporating a Yukawa-type fifth force.

text: During the 1870s, Boltzmann developed a logarithmic approach to quantifying how thermodynamic entropy relates to the microscopic state probabilities of a given system. Gibbs subsequently generalized his entropic framework across the full phase space of many-body systems, while von Neumann adapted it for quantum mechanical contexts. Shannon later employed this same formalism in information theory. For a discrete collection of $W$ microscopic states, the most basic version of this functional reads $S_{BG}= -k\sum_{i=1}^W p_i \ln p_i$ (where $k$ denotes a positive universal constant; {\it BG} refers to {\it Boltzmann-Gibbs}). From this expression emerges the framework of BG statistical mechanics. Although the BG approach has generated countless significant results and practical uses, its application to physical phenomena assumes that {\it ergodicity} holds true. Yet {\it how should we proceed when ergodicity and comparable basic assumptions break down?}—a situation that frequently occurs across numerous natural, engineered, and social systems exhibiting complexity. In 1988, researchers proposed generalizing BG statistical mechanics via a hierarchy of nonadditive entropy functions: $S_q=k\frac{1-\sum_{i=1}^W p_i^q}{q-1}$, which reduces to the traditional additive $S_{BG}$ entropy when $q \to 1$. The parameter $q$ should ideally be derived from underlying mechanical principles. Over the following thirty years, this concept developed extensively across the scientific world (consult Bibliography at \url{http://tsallis.cat.cbpf.br/biblio.htm}), yielding numerous theoretical predictions, experimental confirmations, and implementations spanning physics and beyond. As typically occurs with major {\it paradigm shifts}}, certain debate has understandably arisen within the field. This account surveys the current state of this framework, tracing its path from dynamical and thermodynamic underpinnings through to its most cutting-edge applications in physics.
label: ai

text: Proposed in 2004 and subsequently observed empirically in 2005, the so-called $q$-triplets prompted researchers to hypothesize in 2005 that these entities might embody a comprehensive infinite algebraic structure grounded in the interplay between two self-dual mappings: $q \to 2-q$ (termed {\it additive duality}) and $q \to 1/q$ (termed {\it multiplicative duality}). This algebraic framework contracts to the unique invariant point $q=1$, which aligns with the Boltzmann-Gibbs entropy formalism and classical statistical mechanics. When $q \ne 1$, an unbounded collection of $q$ values manifests, each potentially corresponding to distinct physical attributes of complex systems that admit description through $q$-statistics. The central hypothesis suggests that within any given universality class, only a handful (ordinarily one or two) of independent $q$ parameters are fundamental; all remaining indices derive from these core parameters via algebraic operations. The $q$-triplets appear to represent particularly significant algebraic components. Over recent years, numerous $q$-triplet instances have emerged across theoretical analyses, numerical simulations, laboratory investigations, and field observations spanning natural, engineered, and societal domains. While certain findings align with the algebra constructed via additive and multiplicative dualities alone, others deviate from these predictions. We here extend both dualities with the intent of accommodating a broader spectrum of phenomena. Our generalization pivots on the {\it selfdual} transformation $q \to q_a(q) \equiv \frac{(a+2) -aq}{a-(a-2)q} \,\, (a \in {\cal R})$. We demonstrate that $q_a(1)=1$, that $q_2(q)=2-q$, and that $q_0(q)=1/q$. To provide physical grounding for this extension, we outline illustrative implementations of $q$-statistics to identify systems where the proposed generalized algebras might offer practical utility.

text: This work examines persistent alterations in the orbital behavior of a compact gravitationally bound two-body system $S$ that arise from post-Newtonian tidal forces exerted by a distant massive perturber. The focus is on configurations comprising a small mass $m$ in rapid circular motion around a central body of mass $M$ at angular frequency $n_{\rm b}$, while that central body itself moves gradually in orbit around a far-away mass $M^{'}$ with much slower angular frequency $n_{\rm b}^{'}\ll n_{\rm b}$. Non-Keplerian modulations affecting both $m$ and $M$ are presumed to operate at frequencies significantly lower than either $n_{\rm b}$ or $n_{\rm b}^{'}$. Analytical formulas describing both Newtonian and post-Newtonian tidal perturbations to the orbit of $m$ are derived. The upcoming BepiColombo and JUICE space missions targeting Mercury and Ganymede respectively serve as observational test cases. The most pronounced signatures, ranging from roughly $0.1-0.5$ milliarcseconds annually (mas yr$^{-1}$), emerge for JUICE's Ganymede satellite probe. While refinements in space vehicle position tracking and orbit reconstruction could potentially achieve adequate precision, contamination from numerous additional orbital disturbances of both classical and relativistic nature would prove prohibitively large. An alternative approach using a purpose-built miniature celestial system mounted aboard an Earth-orbiting satellite is also examined. Post-Newtonian tidal precessions reaching $\approx 1-10^2$ mas yr$^{-1}$ appear feasible, yet the substantially stronger Newtonian tidal component would introduce considerable systematic error because present measurements of Earth's mass and gravitational constant carry roughly percent-level uncertainties.

text: We investigate a general relativistic gravitomagnetic effect in a three-body configuration, where the spin angular momentum ${\boldsymbol S}_\textrm{X}$ of a spinning mass $M_\textrm{X}$ perturbs a tightly bound two-body system $\mathcal{S}$ (with characteristic scale $r\ll r_\textrm{X}$) orbiting at separation $r_\textrm{X}$. This system comprises a small test mass moving around a central body $M$. Using post-Newtonian approximation at leading order, we derive analytical expressions for the time-averaged secular evolution of the test particle's Keplerian orbital parameters, revealing persistent modifications to the orbital inclination $I$, ascending node $\Omega$, and argument of periapsis $\omega$. Numerical simulations of a model three-body system validate these analytical predictions. We also estimate the magnitude of such relativistic gravitomagnetic perturbations across various solar system scenarios. For hypothetical artificial satellites orbiting Saturn's moon Enceladus or Jupiter's moon Europa, the relativistic precession rates driven by the spin of these gas giants can reach approximately $10-50~\textrm{milliarcseconds~per~year}~\left(\textrm{mas~yr}^{-1}\right)$. Preliminary numerical modeling suggests that for specific orbital geometries of a hypothetical Europa orbiter, the range-rate residual $\Delta\dot\rho$ may exceed the current Doppler precision capability of the Juno spacecraft, namely $\sigma_{\dot\rho}=0.015~\textrm{mm~s}^{-1}$, within one day. By contrast, perturbations from the Sun's rotation on spacecraft in orbit around Mercury or Earth produce effects only on the order of $1-0.1~\textrm{microarcseconds~per~year}~\left(\mu\textrm{as~yr}^{-1}\right)$.

text: A fundamental difficulty in describing intricate systems across multiple disciplines involves assessing whether increasingly sophisticated natural systems can be analyzed with comparable scientific objectivity and generalizability to the simpler systems studied through classical Newtonian frameworks. This core issue stems from the challenge of accounting for intricate, numerous interactions among system components—a problem remaining unresolved within conventional theoretical approaches. We present here a complete causal resolution of this problem, along with the resulting complexity framework and its practical implementations. Two pivotal characteristics—dynamic multiplicity and interconnectedness—emerge as essential properties, generating a fundamentally novel mathematical formalism that precisely characterizes actual system performance. The enlarged mathematical apparatus underlying complexity theory yields a rigorously universal characterization of dynamic complexity, along with precise definitions of randomness and unpredictability, a comprehensive taxonomy of all conceivable dynamic behaviors, and a fundamental integrating concept—the symmetry principle governing complexity across all system types. Every physical system exhibits a substantial degree of inherent dynamic complexity that remains undiminished, significantly influencing the counterintuitive phenomena observed in quantum mechanics and relativistic phenomena, which become comprehensible as expressions of multifaceted interaction mechanisms. Distinctions observed among disparate systems originate from variations in their particular dynamic complexity characteristics. We discuss how this universal complexity framework applies to systems of pronounced intricacy at elevated organizational tiers—encompassing biological communities, physiological processes, cognitive function, consciousness, networked data systems—and argue that advancement in humanity's institutional and intellectual development requires a fundamental shift toward grasping unreduced complexity (termed a "complexity revolution").

text:
A fundamental component of constraint programming frameworks involves constraint propagation algorithms. This work presents a unified and broadly applicable methodology for systematically describing various constraint propagation techniques. Our approach unfolds across two distinct phases. Initially, we formulate a general-purpose iterative procedure applicable to partial orders, establishing its validity within an abstract context. Subsequently, we specialize this procedure by introducing concrete partial orders and specific operations, thereby deriving concrete constraint propagation algorithms.

By leveraging the principles of commutativity and semi-commutativity, we demonstrate that the algorithms {\tt AC-3}, {\tt PC-2}, {\tt DAC}, and {\tt DPC}—which are designed to attain (directional) arc and (directional) path consistency—represent different instantiations of one overarching generic procedure. The findings presented herein build upon and streamline earlier work by Apt \citeyear{Apt99b}.

text: We present a construction of an algorithm that achieves asymptotically optimal speed—within a factor of 5—for precisely specified computational problems. The core approach involves systematically generating all provably correct programs for the given problem by iterating through all possible proofs. This method can be viewed as both an extension and refinement of Levin search, which represents the asymptotically optimal strategy for function inversion up to constant factors. By restricting consideration to programs accompanied by formal correctness proofs, the limitations imposed by Blum's acceleration theorem are circumvented. Additionally, we demonstrate that the most efficient program solving a particular function necessarily belongs to the class of shortest programs that provably solve it. To formalize this observation, we generalize the notion of Kolmogorov complexity and introduce two novel, intuitive complexity measures applicable to functions.

text: We introduce an innovative and broadly applicable method for incrementally discovering a universal algorithm capable of addressing each problem in a succession of related problems. text:
This work describes a simulated experimental environment that incorporates five distinct architectures for governing animats: a rule-driven approach, an action-oriented architecture, a representation-based framework, a connectionist model, and a Braitenberg-style design. By conducting a series of comparative experiments, I demonstrate that no single architecture emerges as universally superior; rather, each excels within particular domains and specific situations.

The selected architectures, despite their relative simplicity, embody divergent methodologies for investigating cognitive phenomena. Drawing on experimental findings as an empirical tool for philosophical inquiry, I argue that no single methodology for examining cognition can claim universal superiority, given that each carries both strengths and limitations—each illuminates distinct facets of cognition from particular vantage points. This observation bears relevance to ongoing scholarly discussions regarding which methodologies represent the "correct" approach to cognition: while all methodologies possess merit, none achieves complete adequacy on its own. I offer observations regarding how cognition might be conceptualized when drawing insights from the full range of investigative methods, and I present a basic typology distinguishing various forms of cognitive activity.

text: This work tackles the challenge of categorizing instances where feature characteristics depend on contextual factors, particularly when the evaluation dataset contains contexts absent from the training data. The paper first establishes a formal framework for the issue, followed by the presentation of broad methodologies aimed at boosting classifier effectiveness in such scenarios. These methodologies are subsequently evaluated across three distinct application areas. The initial application involves identifying faults in gas turbine engines, where the objective is to pinpoint a defective engine under one set of conditions—for example, warm temperatures—despite the malfunction being previously observed only under different conditions, such as cold temperatures. The second application pertains to automated speech processing, where speaker identity constitutes the contextual dimension. Here, the challenge involves identifying spoken utterances from speakers not included in the original training data. The third application concerns healthcare prediction, specifically forecasting patient survival outcomes in hepatitis cases, with patient age serving as the contextual variable. Across all three applications, accounting for context leads to markedly superior classification outcomes.

text: We propose an approach for categorizing new evidence within the Dempster-Shafer framework. Our technique builds upon earlier research involving the grouping of ambiguous evidence and its transformation into more specific forms. Rather than performing complete re-clustering whenever new evidence arrives, our approach compares incoming data against representative cluster prototypes for rapid assignment. This strategy offers computational efficiency, requiring O(M * N) operations per new evidence item, where M represents the upper bound on subset count and N denotes the number of selected prototypes per subset. Notably, this complexity remains constant regardless of how much evidence has previously been collected. The values of M and N are ordinarily determined in advance and tailored to the particular problem domain.

text: This work examines a Dempster-Shafer theoretical challenge wherein 2**n - 1 items of evidence are partitioned into n groups through a neural network architecture. The partitioning process involves reducing a metaconflict objective function. An earlier approach utilizing iterative optimization techniques was previously introduced. The neural architecture proved to be substantially more efficient than iterative optimization when addressing larger-scale cases. Although the neural architecture exhibited faster metaconflict escalation relative to iterative optimization for intermediate-sized instances, the ratio of metaconflict to both cluster count and evidence quantity remained acceptable. The practice of state aggregation, along with the broader field of feature-based reinforcement learning, involves transforming raw histories and original states into simplified or consolidated representations. The shared objective of these approaches is to produce a condensed system that approximately behaves as a compact, time-invariant finite-state MDP, amenable to efficient resolution or acquisition. We substantially broaden previous aggregation findings by demonstrating that even when the condensed system fails to constitute an MDP, the (q-)value functions and (optimal) policies stemming from a related MDP of equivalent cardinality can still resolve the underlying problem—provided the answer is roughly expressible in relation to the aggregated states. This yields a constraint on the minimum state space dimensionality that applies across all RL scenarios uniformly. Additionally, this could shed light on why RL techniques built specifically for MDPs sometimes achieve success in non-MDP contexts.
label: ai

text: Galles and Pearl contended that "recursive causal models impose no additional constraints on counterfactuals beyond those already established by Lewis's [possible-worlds] approach." This assertion warrants careful investigation to establish precisely how causal models relate to Lewis's framework. It emerges that recursive models map exactly onto a specific subset of (possible-world) counterfactual structures. However, a broader variant of recursive models—specifically those in which all equations possess unique solutions—proves neither comparable nor incomparable in its capacity to express counterfactual structures, even though the Galles-Pearl reasoning would seemingly extend to these cases as well. The flaw in the Galles-Pearl line of reasoning becomes apparent: they dismissed a particular axiom as inconsequential, claiming it relied on disjunction (absent from their vocabulary), when in fact this axiom carries substantial weight.
label: ai

text: We introduce an online variant of the classical cake cutting problem, wherein participants join and exit throughout the resource distribution process. We demonstrate that celebrated fair allocation methods—specifically cut-and-choose and the Dubins-Spanier moving knife technique—can be modified to handle such dynamic scenarios. We establish fairness criteria applicable to online cake cutting mechanisms, including online analogues of proportionality and envy-freeness. Additionally, we examine how agent collusion affects these procedures. We further investigate both theoretically and practically the competitive ratios achieved by these online mechanisms. Our findings indicate that the online cut-and-choose method outperforms its moving knife counterpart, owing to superior collusion resistance and empirically demonstrated effectiveness.

text: This work generalizes Meek's conjecture (Meek 1997), originally formulated for directed acyclic graphs, to the broader framework of chain graphs, and establishes that this generalization holds. We demonstrate that whenever a chain graph H serves as an independence map for the independence model of a chain graph G, two conditions are satisfied: (i) a sequence of operations—specifically, additions of directed and undirected edges along with valid splits and merges—can convert G into H, and (ii) H maintains its status as an independence map of G's independence model throughout each step of this transformation process. This finding carries significant implications for chain graph learning from empirical data, paralleling the impact that Chickering's 2002 proof of Meek's conjecture had for Bayesian network learning: it enables the construction of learning algorithms that are both computationally efficient and asymptotically consistent under reasonable conditions.

text: We introduce a novel information-theoretic quantity termed "uprooted information" in this work. A key finding is that the identifiability of a causal probability $P(s|do(t))$ (in Pearl's sense) relative to a directed acyclic graph $G$ holds if and only if the uprooted information remains non-negative across all instantiations consistent with $G$. Additionally, we develop a computational procedure for semi-Markovian Bayesian networks that determines whether a causal probability $P(s|do(t))$ can be identified, and when identification is possible, reformulates it in terms devoid of unobserved confounders. This method extends and refines an earlier approach due to Tian and Pearl, addressing what appears to be a minor deficiency in their framework. Furthermore, we establish both a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for identifiability of $P(s|do(t))$ in cases where $t$ comprises a single variable. Previous work in this area has only furnished a sufficient graphical criterion for such scenarios. By "graphical," we refer to conditions derived directly from Judea Pearl's three do-calculus postulates.

text: A recent proposal by Halpern and Leung uses weighted collections of probability measures to characterize uncertainty, along with a decision-making framework centered on weighted regret maximization. The definition naturally extends orderings derived from standard probability and lower probability, with an analogous extension available for upper probability. Additionally, we provide a complete set of axioms that fully characterize this conception of likelihood based on regret.

text: We introduce PeerRank, a novel approach to evaluating peers through assessment. The method derives an agent's grade by aggregating the evaluations submitted by peers assessing that agent. Since each peer's grade reflects their competence at grading, PeerRank amplifies the influence of assessments from more reliable evaluators. This creates a mechanism that motivates peers to provide accurate evaluations. Because an agent's score depends on their assessors' scores, which in turn rely on other assessors' scores, we formalize PeerRank through a recursive equation analogous to PageRank's approach for ordering web pages. We examine key mathematical characteristics of PeerRank (including conformity to unanimity, no dummy, no discrimination, and symmetry principles), present illustrative cases, situate it within existing literature, and test it against synthetic benchmarks. Our findings demonstrate substantial effectiveness, often cutting grade prediction error in half or better relative to the straightforward approach of taking the mean of all peer grades.
label: ai

text: This text examines a causality framework developed by Halpern and Pearl that relies on structural equations. Building on this foundation, it then explores an enhanced formulation that incorporates considerations of normalcy and commonality—factors widely recognized as influential in how causal relationships are attributed. Traditionally, causality operates as a binary concept: either A causes B, or it does not. The discussion goes on to review an expansion of the causality definition put forth by Chockler and Halpern, introducing graduated notions of agent responsibility and culpability. To illustrate: when someone secures an election by a vote of 11-0, each supporting voter bears less responsibility for the outcome compared to a scenario where the margin was 6-5. The concept of graduated blame incorporates an agent's knowledge and beliefs. In essence, an agent A's degree of blame for outcome B represents the anticipated level of responsibility of A for B, computed across what the agent knows. The text concludes by contrasting the structural-equations approach to causality with Wright's NESS criterion.

text: Quantum neural computing represents an integrated perspective on how the brain operates. This framework proposes that classical information processing takes place within neural architecture, while the virtual particles tied to the dynamic states of these structures establish the fundamental quantum substrate. We examine this framework afresh and discuss emerging perspectives on three distinct mechanisms through which the brain learns from environmental stimuli: associative, reorganizational, and quantum modes. Associative and reorganizational learning mechanisms have strong empirical support in experimental data; demonstrating that the brain exhibits quantum characteristics as a unified system proves considerably more challenging. We contend that the brain's reorganizational capacity may be understood as self-adjustment occurring at the quantum level of the overall system. Beyond just the neural substrates themselves, their abstract conceptualizations likewise warrant treatment as integrated wholes. We explore the complementary pairs inherent in brain function and examine the ways these opposing aspects connect and interact.

text:
Two distinct five-valued formulations for neutrosophic entropy are presented in this work. The first builds upon Kaufmann's original approach, while the second extends Kosko's foundational model.

Drawing from fundamental three-valued information composed of truth degree, falsity degree, and neutrality degree, several five-valued formulations have been developed to better illuminate particular characteristics of neutrosophic entropy. Specifically, five dimensions of neutrosophic uncertainty are identified: ambiguity, ignorance, contradiction, neutrality, and saturation. These five uncertainty dimensions are then expanded into a seven-part partition of unity through the incorporation of two additional neutrosophic certainty dimensions: truth and falsity.

Furthermore, this work examines specialized instantiations of neutrosophic entropy corresponding to bifuzzy systems, intuitionistic fuzzy systems, paraconsistent fuzzy systems, and conventional fuzzy systems.

text: According to Gray's 2015 work, the Fermi paradox (FP) should not be considered a genuine paradox and represents a misleading designation. Gray further contended that Fermi himself did not originate this concept—his casual luncheon conversation actually focused on the practical challenges of space travel rather than the question of alien life. Gray credits Hart and Tipler as the true architects of the paradox, while simultaneously proposing that it lacks scientific legitimacy and therefore has no place in SETI-related discussions. However, these claims suffer from weak reasoning, lack historical rigor, and in certain cases depend on overly rigid and ungenerous interpretations of available data. I propose three counterarguments instead: (i) Regardless of Gray's claims, the historical attribution of concepts bears no connection to their intellectual validity. (ii) The Great Silence paradox represents a natural extension of FP, making any attempt to treat them as distinct concepts neither theoretically sound nor empirically justified. (iii) Contrary to Gray's central thesis, FP has actually intensified in recent times as astrobiology has advanced and expanded our understanding.

text:
This paper examines multiple methods from existing literature for organizing and breaking down Dempster-Shafer collective belief functions, with particular focus on their ability to represent independence relationships—given the perspective that belief functions extend the Bayesian probability framework.

The analysis shows that logical networks developed by Zhu and Lee and the directed acyclic graphs proposed by Smets both fail to adequately model the statistical dependencies and independencies found in Bayesian networks as described by Pearl. In contrast, the hypergraph model introduced by Shenoy and Shafer successfully portrays how Bayesian belief functions factor according to Bayesian network structure, yet the authors maintain that representing variable independence in belief function contexts is unnecessary.

Cano and colleagues challenge the Shenoy-Shafer hypergraph approach specifically because it omits variable independence representation. However, their proposed alternative framework cannot decompose all belief functions that are decomposable within the Shenoy-Shafer system.

A competing strategy presented in the Klopotek framework achieves a synthesis of these competing perspectives. It maintains the simplest possible factorization for belief functions under the Shenoy-Shafer scheme, while simultaneously capturing all variable independencies recognized by the Cano approach—plus additional ones beyond their scope.

text:
The Dempster-Shafer Theory, alternatively referred to as the Mathematical Theory of Evidence (DST), serves as a fundamental framework for conducting inference when information is characterized by differing degrees of specificity. Despite considerable scholarly attention devoted to developing this theory since its inception, numerous unresolved issues persist. Among the most significant of these unresolved matters is how frequencies relate to the Mathematical Theory of Evidence. A persistent criticism holds that the theory systematically excludes frequencies from its conceptual scope. This critique carries substantial weight for two principal reasons: (1) empirical studies cannot be conducted to validate the effectiveness of DST-based representations of phenomena in the actual world relative to observed evidence, and (2) empirical observations cannot form the basis for designing appropriate models of belief.

  This work presents a frequency-based framework for understanding DST that effectively rebuts the preceding objection. A key implication of this approach is the feasibility of creating computational procedures that automatically construct DST belief models on the basis of empirical evidence. We introduce three distinct procedures suited to different types of belief model configurations: those organized as tree structures, those arranged as poly-tree structures, and those comprising unrestricted network topologies.
